questions.question: Why do neural circuits (neurons plus glia, which are helper cells) produce new functions not seen in single neurons?

options.A: Because a single neuron can do any complex brain job by itself.
options.B: Because complex, changing connections and signals among cells create new patterns only when they act together.
options.C: Because glial helper cells do most electrical signaling, so neuron properties don't matter.
options.D: Because adding more neurons just averages differences, so no new behaviors appear.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened wording, used one clear question sentence, defined glia in parentheses, and simplified technical terms (nonlinear, topology, plasticity) to 'complex, changing connections and signals'.
concepts_tested: Emergent properties of neural circuits: how complex functions arise from interactions among neurons and glia beyond individual components.

Levels of analysis and cross-scale integration: studying the nervous system from molecular/cellular scales to imaging and cognitive tasks, and how each scale contributes to understanding brain function.

Multidisciplinary framework and scope: the role of integrating physiology, anatomy, molecular biology, psychology, physics, computer science, and more to understand learning, memory, behavior, perception, and consciousness.

questions.question: Why can people do familiar tasks very quickly? How does the difference between conscious (controlled) and unconscious (automatic) processing explain this?

options.A: Automatic processing requires conscious effort and adapts instantly to new demands.
options.B: Automatic processing works without conscious control and uses practiced associations for fast responses. Controlled processing needs focused attention and rules, so it flexibly adapts when habits fail.
options.C: Both processing modes use the same attention, so speed is the same for familiar and new tasks.
options.D: Conscious processing only handles movements, while unconscious processing handles thinking; this explains speed versus adaptation.

questions.simplification_notes: Split the question into two short sentences, used simple words, and kept the key contrast between automatic (fast) and controlled (flexible) processing.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Cognitive processes are functionally categorized (perception, attention, memory, thinking, language) and interact to acquire, transform, store, retrieve, and use information.
Concept 2: The distinction between conscious vs unconscious and controlled vs automatic processing as a fundamental principle shaping how cognitive processes unfold.

Concept 3: Competing theoretical frameworks (classical computationalism, connectionism, representationalism/anti-representationalism) as models for explaining how cognition operates and represents information.

questions.question: Why are mental states and behavior described as "emergent" from the brain instead of caused by one neuron? Emergent means system-level traits that appear when many parts work together.

options.A: Mental states are identical to single neuron firings, so there is no emergence.
options.B: Many neurons interacting create system-level properties single neurons can't predict, so mind and behavior emerge from the brain.
options.C: Mental states are only social ideas and do not depend on brain activity.
options.D: Behavior comes only from outside stimuli and the brain has no causal role.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened wording, added a simple definition of "emergent," used plain language and kept the original correct choice B.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: The mind-brain-behavior relationship and the idea that brain processes give rise to mental states and observable behavior (emergent properties linking biology to psychology).
Concept 2: The role of empirical methods in psychology to infer causal and correlational relationships among psychosocial variables.

Concept 3: The interdisciplinary and applied nature of psychology, connecting natural and social sciences and guiding applications across clinical, organizational, educational, and other real-world domains.

questions.question: You set the significance level to $\alpha=0.05$ and then collect a larger sample. How will Type I and Type II error rates change, and why?

options.A: Type I error rises with sample size; Type II error also rises because more data gives more chances to reject the null.
options.B: Type I stays about $\alpha=0.05$ (if test assumptions hold); Type II decreases because larger samples cut variability and raise power.
options.C: Type I falls near zero with more data; Type II stays the same, since more data only makes the null easier to disprove.
options.D: Both Type I and Type II errors fall in proportion to $\sqrt{n}$, because more data always makes decisions more accurate.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened sentences, explained $\alpha=0.05$, kept terms 'Type I/II error' and 'power'; removed technical details.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Population vs. sample and the importance of representative sampling for valid inferences (how sampling design affects generalization).
Concept 2: Descriptive vs. inferential statistics and the role of probability theory in drawing conclusions from data (why variation in data necessitates inference).
Concept 3: Hypothesis testing framework and error types (null vs. alternative hypotheses; Type I and Type II errors; how tests inform conclusions).

questions.question: Why do scientists use systems-level models to explain new behaviors in living networks? Why not study each gene or protein alone?

options.A: Emergent behaviors are just the sum of parts, so models are not needed.
options.B: New behaviors come from feedback and complex interactions between parts. Models show these links and let scientists test changes.
options.C: They are caused only by environmental noise, so models just complicate things.
options.D: Measuring each component at steady state is enough; dynamics and interactions can be ignored.

questions.simplification_notes: Split into two short sentences, simplified wording, replaced technical terms with 'feedback' and 'interactions', kept original meaning and correct answer.
concepts_tested: Emergence: how system-level properties arise from dynamic interactions among genes, proteins, and metabolites, and why modeling is needed to understand these properties.
Holistic integration: the necessity of combining quantitative data across multiple components and disciplines to build predictive, mechanism-based models of biological networks.
Iterative modeling cycle: the continual loop of theory/analytical modeling to generate hypotheses, experimental validation, and refinement of models to better describe cellular processes.

questions.question: How can loss of genetic diversity in one plant species change the whole ecosystem?

options.A: It directly lowers species richness in the area.
options.B: It weakens the plant population's ability to adapt to pests and new conditions. This causes changes in herbivores, pollinators, and decomposers, altering productivity and nutrient cycling.
options.C: It causes immediate speciation and raises overall biodiversity.
options.D: It has no effect beyond individual plants.

questions.simplification_notes: Made the question one short sentence and rewrote the answer choices into brief, clear phrases or two short sentences. Kept the scientific idea that reduced genetic diversity lowers adaptability and causes ripple effects through species and ecosystem processes.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: The multi-level nature of biodiversity (genetic, species, ecosystem, phylogenetic) and how changes at one level can influence others.
Concept 2: Latitudinal and climate-driven gradients in biodiversity, particularly why the tropics host higher diversity due to warm climate and high primary productivity.

Concept 3: Anthropogenic drivers of biodiversity loss (habitat destruction, climate change, overhunting) and the resulting Holocene/sixth mass extinction, including connections to past mass extinction events.

questions.question: In the policy cycle, evaluation checks if a policy worked. How does this result usually affect the next stage?

options.A: It makes the policy stop at once, without checking other options.
options.B: It forces one policy tool to be kept no matter the results or feedback.
options.C: It shows what worked and what failed. Policymakers then adjust goals, redesign tools, or send it back to agenda-setting or formulation.
options.D: It guarantees permanent legitimacy and ends the whole cycle.

questions.simplification_notes: Language shortened and split into two short sentences; removed academic jargon; kept core idea that evaluation gives evidence and can send policy back to earlier stages for revision.
concepts_tested: The policy cycle as a framework linking agenda setting, formulation, legitimation, implementation, and evaluation.
The role of multiple actors and stakeholders in policy formation, including co-production with communities and experts.
Policy instruments and aims: how regulations, subsidies, taxes, and spending are used to achieve social/economic objectives.

questions.question: How does a formal governing body help a group adapt to outside pressures? Examples of groups are a state, a company, or an NGO.

options.A: Formal bodies set enforceable rules, clear authority, and legitimacy. This enables consistent decisions and accountability across contexts.
options.B: Formality is mostly ceremonial; real results depend on group culture, not structure.
options.C: Outside pressures always override internal governance, so formality does not matter.
options.D: Informal groups adapt better automatically because they avoid rigid rules.

questions.simplification_notes: Split into two short sentences, removed heavy jargon, and kept the core idea that formal bodies provide rules, authority, and legitimacy enabling consistent adaptation.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Governance as a framework of rules, norms, and processes that shape conduct and decision-making within a group.
Concept 2: Mechanisms of governance—enforcement of rules, allocation of resources, and setting direction—that translate governance into outcomes.
Concept 3: The role and formality of governing bodies and power dynamics, including how governance adapts across different entities (state, corporations, NGOs) and interacts with external influences.

questions.question: How does teaching students to think for themselves change how education avoids indoctrination? How does it help create new knowledge?

options.A: It guarantees students will find the truth by thinking alone, no matter the evidence.
options.B: It helps students judge evidence, justify ideas, and change beliefs when new facts appear, reducing indoctrination and creating new knowledge.
options.C: It removes the need to show many viewpoints because reasoning will find one universal truth.
options.D: It makes teachers decide all acceptable conclusions, which lowers students' independence.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened wording, split into two short sentences, simplified vocabulary, kept focus on reasoning vs. transmission, indoctrination, and knowledge creation.
concepts_tested: Epistemic aims of education: the debate over whether education should prioritize transmitting true beliefs or developing students' reasoning and new knowledge, including the role of critical thinking vs. indoctrination.

Education, power, and equality: how states or societal structures influence education (e.g., coercion to attend school) and issues of discrimination and wealth distribution affecting educational access and outcomes.

Curriculum content, pedagogy, and research methodology: how decisions about what to teach (sex education, religion, moral/art/science education) and how to teach it reflect philosophical assumptions, alongside debates between quantitative and qualitative educational research approaches.

questions.question: Which reason best explains why school systems often keep social class differences instead of reducing them?

options.A: The formal curriculum is fair and gives equal outcomes to all students.
options.B: Tracking and unequal access to quality teachers and resources give already advantaged students steady advantages, while others fall behind.
options.C: Schools use policies that deliberately remove class, race, and gender hierarchies so achievement is the same for everyone.
options.D: Peer culture in schools cancels family background effects by promoting shared norms and values.

questions.simplification_notes: Made the question shorter and simpler. Kept key idea that tracking and unequal resources cause cumulative advantage. Options were shortened and kept plausible.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Education as both a potential engine of meritocratic progress and a mechanism for reproducing social inequality (mobility vs reproduction).
Concept 2: Schooling patterns Reflecting and reinforcing social stratification (class, race, gender) rather than challenging it.

Concept 3: Competing theoretical frameworks (functionalism, human-capital theory, neo-Marxism) that explain education’s role in modern industrial societies (cohesion, efficiency, control, and reproduction).

questions.question: A scarce resource can be used in several ways. How do higher prices decide which uses get the resource, and why is this usually efficient in a competitive market?

options.A: Higher prices exhaust supply, so the resource goes to uses with the lowest production costs, ignoring demand.
options.B: Higher prices make all users cut consumption roughly the same, keeping shares the same but lowering total use.
options.C: Higher prices signal scarcity and shift the resource to uses with the highest willingness to pay (biggest extra benefit), aligning marginal benefit with marginal cost.
options.D: Higher prices always remove externalities and make everyone better off.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened wording into two simple sentences, used clearer words, explained 'willingness to pay' as 'extra benefit', and kept the core price-allocation and efficiency idea.
concepts_tested: Market mechanisms: how prices coordinate supply and demand to allocate scarce resources across uses.
Market failure and efficiency: conditions under which markets fail to achieve efficient outcomes and potential policy implications.
Utility maximization under constraints: rational choice with preferences, budget sets, and local non-satiation forming the basis of consumer theory.

questions.question: Why can higher output per worker not mean the whole economy is more efficient if TFP stays unchanged? For example, capital deepening (more machines per worker) might raise output per worker.

options.A: It must mean all inputs are used more efficiently, so TFP rose.
options.B: More machines per worker (capital deepening) can raise output per worker even if overall efficiency (TFP) is unchanged.
options.C: Living standards automatically improve because output per worker rises.
options.D: It proves a measurement error; partial measures are always biased.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened wording to two clear sentences, explained 'capital deepening' in parentheses, kept technical idea that partial (labour) productivity can mislead about total efficiency (TFP).
concepts_tested: The ratio-based definition of productivity and how aggregation of outputs/inputs affects what is measured
Partial productivity versus total productivity and the interpretive limitations of focusing on a single input
The relationship between productivity growth and economic outcomes such as living standards and firm profitability

questions.question: Alleles A and a have $p=q=0.5$, so Hardy–Weinberg predicts $AA:Aa:aa=0.25:0.50:0.25$. If mating is assortative (like-with-like) with no selection, migration, or drift, what happens next generation and why?

options.A: They stay $0.25:0.50:0.25$ because Hardy–Weinberg holds without selection, migration, or drift.
options.B: More homozygotes (more AA and aa, fewer Aa) but allele frequencies stay about the same because like-with-like mating reshuffles genotypes.
options.C: More heterozygotes (more Aa) because assortative mating creates new allele combinations each generation.
options.D: Genotype frequencies change unpredictably due to random effects, so no clear pattern can be predicted.

questions.simplification_notes: Combined background and question into two short sentences, used LaTeX for frequencies, simplified wording and kept the core idea that assortative mating raises homozygosity without changing allele frequencies.
concepts_tested: Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium as the baseline for allele frequency stability and the conditions under which variation is maintained or altered
Polygenic basis of continuous variation and how selection acts on many discrete genes to drive evolution
Roles of mutation, genetic drift, and recombination (and linkage disequilibrium) in shaping genetic variation and evolutionary trajectories

questions.question: Why does the capability approach separate "functionings" from "capabilities"? How does that change how we judge people's well-being?

options.A: Functionings are actual achievements; capabilities are real freedoms to achieve them. This makes us judge people's opportunities and fairness.
options.B: Functionings show potential opportunities and capabilities show outcomes; so we should focus on what people could do.
options.C: Capabilities are resources and functionings are social norms. So well-being should be based on owning resources.
options.D: Functionings and capabilities mean the same thing, so choosing one or the other does not change evaluation.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened sentences, removed technical detail, kept the key difference (achievements vs. freedoms) and effect on judging well-being.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Capability vs. functionings and substantive freedoms

Concept 2: Sen’s five components for assessing capability (real freedoms, conversion factors, multi-variate nature of well-being, balance of material and nonmaterial factors, distribution of opportunities)
Concept 3: Relationship to policy measurement and development (HDI, policy paradigm, sustainability considerations)

questions.question: If risk is the effect of uncertainty on objectives, why can risk be both good and bad for reaching goals?

options.A: Because uncertainty only reduces the chance of meeting goals, so risk is always harmful.
options.B: Because uncertainty can move outcomes above or below expectations, making threats or opportunities. Risk shows deviations from objectives, not just losses.
options.C: Because risk counts only how likely bad events are, ignoring possible good outcomes.
options.D: Because risk depends only on the chance of a bad event, not on its impact on goals.

questions.simplification_notes: Made the sentence short and plain language; removed technical terms and examples while keeping the core idea that uncertainty can create both threats and opportunities.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Risk as the effect of uncertainty on objectives (risk influences whether objectives are met; can be positive or negative).
Concept 2: Components of risk (risk sources, potential events, their consequences, and their likelihood) that together define risk.
Concept 3: Risk management as a principles/framework-based activity (ISO 31000/ISO 31073) with variation in application across different practice areas.

questions.question: Something $X$ is in environment $E$ and has response options $R$. How do changes in $E$ or $R$ affect $X$'s security?

options.A: Security is a fixed trait of $X$ and does not depend on $E$ or $R$.
options.B: Adding any new capability to $R$ always makes $X$ more secure, no matter the threats in $E$.
options.C: Security depends on how well $R$ matches threats in $E$. If threats rise, $R$ must scale or change to keep $X$ safe.
options.D: Security comes only from the strongest single response in $R$, regardless of the environment $E$.

questions.simplification_notes: Split the complex sentence into two short sentences. Used $X$, $E$, $R$ in LaTeX. Shortened and clarified each option while keeping the original meanings.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Security is a relationship that depends on the referent’s environment and the referent’s own capabilities, i.e., security emerges from interactions between a target, its surroundings, and its response options.

Concept 2: Security capabilities function as mechanisms to achieve or maintain security, including coercive power, protective systems, warning systems, diplomatic/social action, and policy; their roles and interactions explain how insecurity is prevented or mitigated.

Concept 3: The referent concept and scope (who or what is being protected) shapes security policy and discourse, with the possibility of multiple referents (e.g., a nation-state and its citizens) forming a combined security context.

questions.question: Why does the relational-structural view say written rules alone cannot make corporate governance work? What else matters between managers, the board, shareholders and stakeholders?

options.A: Because well-made written rules always make governance effective.
options.B: Because governance needs aligned goals, incentives and monitoring. Power relations and information flow affect how rules are used.
options.C: Because only following laws matters and relationships are not important.
options.D: Because stakeholders have no influence on how a company is run.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened and split the original complex sentence into two clear sentences. Kept technical terms but explained the needed relationships and incentives. Made answer choices brief and plausible.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Governance as a system of directing and controlling a corporation through its structures, processes, and mechanisms.

Concept 2: The relational-structural view of governance, i.e., the set of relationships among management, the board, shareholders, and stakeholders, and how these relations influence objectives and monitoring.
Concept 3: Governance principles and regulatory frameworks (Cadbury/OECD/SOX) that guide governance practices beyond narrow context-specific rules.

questions.question: If land, capital, and entrepreneur are fixed, why does total output rise by smaller amounts when we add more workers?

options.A: Because technology makes each new worker equally productive.
options.B: Because each extra worker adds less output when some inputs are fixed (marginal product falls).
options.C: Because fixed inputs become more efficient as more workers are added, keeping output growth steady.
options.D: Because demand for the product falls as more workers are hired, limiting output.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the sentence, used simpler words, and briefly explained 'marginal product' in parentheses. Kept the original idea of diminishing returns when other inputs are fixed.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Production function and input-output relationship — how the utilized amounts of land, labor, capital, and entrepreneur determine output.

Concept 2: Classification of factors (primary vs secondary) and the role of technology/human capital — how certain inputs are transformed or not transformed in production, and how tech/human capital can be treated as factors.
Concept 3: Distribution of value and costs among factors — how rents and wages arise from factor use and how different economic theories explain the allocation of value and costs to factors.

questions.question: How do strong institutions and better human capital help a poor country grow and reduce poverty?

options.A: They protect property rights and make workers more productive, so investment is safer and more likely.
options.B: They guarantee automatic catch-up to rich-country incomes just by transferring technology.
options.C: They only affect long-run outcomes and do not change short-term poverty or investment.
options.D: They always move resources from productive investment to consumption, reducing capital buildup.

questions.simplification_notes: Made the question shorter and clearer; replaced jargon with simple phrases (institutions → property rights; human capital → worker skills). Kept the key mechanism about risk, productivity, and investment.
concepts_tested: The role of institutions and human capital in shaping development outcomes (why they matter for growth, poverty reduction, and inequality)
Policy instruments and incentive structures as mechanisms that influence development (how restructuring market incentives or using optimization methods affects outcomes)
Convergence vs. non-convergence and the influence of social/political factors on development trajectories (how interdisciplinary factors relate to differing development paths)

questions.question: Why is a shape called symmetric when a transformation moves its parts but keeps the whole the same?

options.A: The whole shape looks the same after the move even though parts swap places.
options.B: Parts must be the same size no matter how they are arranged.
options.C: Symmetry only means the shape gets bigger or smaller but stays similar.
options.D: Symmetry is just how things look, not a precise math rule.

questions.simplification_notes: Made the sentence shorter and clearer for middle school. Kept the idea that symmetry means invariance under transformations that permute parts. Removed advanced terms and extra examples.
concepts_tested: Invariance under transformations as the defining principle of geometric symmetry (a transformation moves parts but preserves the overall shape)
Classification of symmetry by transformation type (reflectional, rotational, translational, glide reflection, rotoreflection, helical, scale; plus fractal scale symmetry)
Symmetry as a relational concept in logic (symmetric relations and symmetric connectives; contrast with non-symmetric operations like implication)

questions.question: Why can we study a surface by itself, without using how it sits in 3D, when using Gaussian curvature? 'Intrinsic' means it depends only on distances and angles on the surface.

options.A: Because Gaussian curvature is determined by distances and angles on the surface. Bendings that keep those distances (isometries) do not change curvature.
options.B: Because Gaussian curvature depends on how the surface sits in 3D, so bending the surface changes its curvature.
options.C: Because intrinsic curvature is about the surrounding space, not the surface itself, so the surface properties don't matter.
options.D: Because all curved surfaces can be flattened without stretching, so how they sit in 3D is not needed to find curvature.

questions.simplification_notes: Made wording simpler and shorter for middle school. Explained 'intrinsic' as depending only on surface distances and angles. Kept technical term 'isometries' but explained it briefly.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Intrinsic versus extrinsic properties of geometric objects (e.g., Gaussian curvature being intrinsic and independent of embedding in Euclidean space; surfaces studied as standalone spaces).
Concept 2: The parallel postulate and non-Euclidean geometries; how relaxing Euclid’s assumptions leads to new geometries and their physical relevance (e.g., general relativity).

Concept 3: Geometry as a unifying framework for spaces and connections to other branches (e.g., algebraic geometry, differential geometry, topology, computation) and the shift from physical space to abstract mathematical spaces (manifolds, Riemannian geometry).

questions.question: How does natural selection make a population change over time?

options.A: Allele frequencies change by random chance, independent of fitness.
options.B: Mating choices change genotype ratios, causing helpful alleles to rise.
options.C: Individuals with heritable traits that help survival and reproduction leave more offspring, so those traits increase.
options.D: Beneficial mutations occur often and quickly replace less fit alleles.

questions.simplification_notes: Question shortened and simplified wording; options kept short and clear; original distractors (genetic drift, nonrandom mating, mutation) preserved; correct answer unchanged.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Evolution by natural selection as the driver of biological diversity and a unifying framework for biology
Concept 2: The cell as the basic unit of life and the role of genes/heredity in inheritance (cellular and genetic basis of life)
Concept 3: Energy transformation and homeostasis as core life processes that govern organism function and ecosystem energy flow

questions.question: A small steady push hits an Earth system with strong positive feedback and a threshold. Why can this push cause a sudden big change instead of a slow one?

options.A: Positive feedback amplifies the push; after the threshold the system flips quickly.
options.B: Negative feedback reduces changes, so the system would respond slowly and smoothly.
options.C: Inertia always prevents abrupt change, so the response stays proportional to the push.
options.D: Random noise alone can cause jumps, so abrupt changes happen without the push.

questions.simplification_notes: Split into two short sentences, removed technical jargon, kept the idea that positive feedback plus a threshold causes abrupt change.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Connectivity and cross-sphere interactions among the Earth’s subsystems
Concept 2: Feedback mechanisms and non-linear dynamics, including potential abrupt changes or thresholds
Concept 3: Variability across space/time and the role of past states/models to forecast future behavior

questions.question: Why do planetary boundaries warn that crossing a limit can cause sudden, big Earth changes instead of small, steady ones?

options.A: Because Earth has feedbacks and tipping points that can turn small pushes into fast, large changes.
options.B: Because stress and response are always smooth and steady, so thresholds only mark slow changes.
options.C: Because people can always fix problems perfectly, so boundaries do not matter for system behavior.
options.D: Because each boundary stands alone and crossing one removes risks from the others.

questions.simplification_notes: Made one short question; replaced 'non-linear, abrupt' with 'sudden, big' and explained feedbacks and tipping points simply; shortened all options to single sentences.
concepts_tested: Safe operating space / Holocene stability as the normative baseline for human activity
Thresholds and non-linear, abrupt changes risk when boundaries are crossed
Human drivers and framework evolution (industrialization as driver; model-based updates and re-naming to emphasize biosphere integrity)

questions.question: How does the history of physics show it mixes with other sciences, and what mainly creates these links?

options.A: Fields split as methods differ, so boundaries harden, driven by experimental limits.
options.B: New physics ideas find general principles that explain other sciences, creating cross-disciplinary research.
options.C: Physics stays isolated because its laws are unique and do not apply elsewhere.
options.D: Only technology needs drive cross-disciplinary work, not theory unification.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the question, used simpler words, kept two-part idea: history shows mixing and asks main driver. Options made concise and plausible.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Interdisciplinary relationships — physics intersects with fields like biophysics and quantum chemistry, and new physics ideas explain mechanisms in other sciences.

Concept 2: Cause-and-effect link between physics and technology — advances in areas such as electromagnetism, thermodynamics, and mechanics drive technological developments (e.g., televisions, industrial processes, calculus).

Concept 3: Evolution and unification in science — the historical shift from natural philosophy to specialized disciplines and the idea that physics’ boundaries are not rigid, with new ideas expanding connections across disciplines.

questions.question: Sellers know their own costs but buyers do not. How does offering a menu of contracts make sellers reveal costs and help the market?

options.A: It makes all sellers face the same payoff, so private cost differences do not matter.
options.B: It creates incentive compatibility: each type gets a higher payoff from the right contract, so they self-select and reveal their cost.
options.C: The designer can see costs after signing, so no information needs to be revealed.
options.D: It gives random payments to equalize payoffs, which accidentally leaks some information.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened wording to two simple sentences, kept core idea of screening and self-selection (incentive compatibility).
concepts_tested: Information asymmetry and contract design: how incomplete information between parties leads to market failures and how mechanism design can elicit information-sharing and welfare-enhancing outcomes.

Information goods economics: the idea that some goods are expensive to produce but cheap to reproduce, creating non-rivalry and underprovision of information-intensive products (e.g., basic research) and justifications for government subsidies.

Information, signaling, and game-theoretic frameworks: viewing information as signals within games (perfect/complete/incomplete information) and using game theory to model information flows and policy design (e.g., screening, signaling, and mechanism design).

questions.question: Which method makes a government policy be carried out the same way by different agencies?

options.A: Direct orders from top leaders that tell frontline staff exactly what to do.
options.B: Standard rules and procedures plus performance standards that guide staff and coordinate agencies.
options.C: Local public debates and community opinions that change how policies are applied.
options.D: Agencies competing like businesses for money and staff.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the question and options to plain language for middle school. Kept the main idea about rules and bureaucracy as the coordinating mechanism.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Translation of politics into public action (how policies are implemented and how administrative processes connect decision-making to citizens’ everyday experiences)
Concept 2: Bureaucracy as a mechanism for public administration (Weberian ideas about organized structures, standardization, and rational decision-making)
Concept 3: Democratic/normative foundations shaping administration (the role of leadership, dignity of citizens, justice, liberty, and opportunity in guiding public management)

questions.question: Why do social scientists use numbers, interviews, and computer methods together? How does mixing these methods give stronger explanations than using one method alone?

options.A: It increases sample size to reach statistical significance.
options.B: It lets researchers check findings across data types, reveal different parts of a problem, and offset each method's weaknesses.
options.C: It makes all studies use the same procedures.
options.D: It guarantees results will apply in every context.

questions.simplification_notes: Question shortened to two clear sentences using simple words. 'Triangulation' explained as checking findings across data types. Technical ideas kept but simplified.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Epistemological divide shaping research method (positivist vs interpretivist)
Concept 2: Methodological pluralism and interdisciplinarity (combining quantitative/qualitative methods, and using big data/computational tools)
Concept 3: Autonomy and evolution of social science as a field (expanding beyond sociology, shared goals/methods across disciplines)

questions.question: How do social structures and people's choices work together to make society stable or cause change?

options.A: Structures fully control behaviour; people cannot change society.
options.B: People's choices alone make society; structures do not matter.
options.C: Structures shape and limit actions, but many repeated individual actions can add up and change structures.
options.D: Social order appears on its own without people or structures interacting.

questions.simplification_notes: Reworded 'individual agency' as 'people's choices' and used simpler sentences. Kept the key idea that structures constrain actions while repeated actions can change structures.
concepts_tested: The interplay between social structure and individual agency as a mechanism shaping social order and change
The micro-level vs macro-level scales of analysis and how phenomena at different levels relate
The impact of digital technologies on social behavior, networks, and institutions (digital sociology) as a mechanism linking technology to social outcomes

questions.question: Why do new system-level properties appear when parts interact?

options.A: Because each part's effects cancel out, leaving only the average traits of parts.
options.B: Because local interactions can create feedback that amplifies or reduces effects, making global patterns one part cannot predict.
options.C: Because the environment fully determines every system outcome, so internal interactions do not matter.
options.D: Because parts act independently, so the system is just the sum of isolated behaviors.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened wording and sentences for middle school level; kept the core idea that interactions and feedback produce unpredictable system-wide patterns.
concepts_tested: Emergence and synergy: How system-level properties arise from interrelations that are not reducible to individual parts.
Interdependence, boundaries, and context: Why changing one component can affect the whole system and how boundaries and environmental context shape system behavior.
Adaptation and equifinality via environmental engagement: How a system’s growth and adaptation depend on engagement with its environment, and why multiple paths can lead to similar outcomes.

questions.question: What happens when people learn and have bounded rationality? How does this combine with institutions (rules, laws, habits) to change those institutions over time?

options.A: They allow fast, perfect redesign of institutions when outside conditions change.
options.B: They cause slow, step-by-step change that follows past paths, as people update expectations inside existing rules.
options.C: They remove institutions and replace them with only formal market rules.
options.D: They make institutions ignore new information and stay the same no matter what.

questions.simplification_notes: Split into two short sentences; explained bounded rationality and institutions in parentheses; simplified option wording while keeping original meanings.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Institutions as determinants of economic behavior and the source of market outcomes (causal mechanism linking institutions to economics)
Concept 2: Evolution, learning, and bounded rationality as dynamic drivers of institutional change (how institutions change over time)

Concept 3: The role of legal foundations and social norms in shaping economic activity, contrasting institutional economics with classical/homo economicus approaches (the framework and mechanisms by which institutions influence incentives and constraints)

questions.question: A value chain is a system of linked activities. Why might improving just one activity not raise total profit?

options.A: Because improving one activity always raises value in all other activities.
options.B: Because total profit depends on how all activities work together; changing one can shift costs or benefits to others.
options.C: Because only the final product's price matters, so changing activities won't affect costs.
options.D: Because activities are independent, so improving one will not affect the rest.

questions.simplification_notes: Split the question into two short sentences, used simpler words, and made each option short and clear while keeping the original idea about system interactions.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: The value chain as a system where each activity adds value and collectively determines costs and profits.
Concept 2: Competitive advantage can be gained by optimizing any one of the five primary activities (inbound logistics, operations, outbound logistics, marketing and sales, service).
Concept 3: The analytical level matters; evaluating at the business unit level, rather than the entire corporation, is necessary to uncover sources of competitive advantage.

questions.question: Emergence is when a system shows new behaviors not predictable from its parts alone. How does emergence happen?

options.A: By simply adding each part's effects together.
options.B: By nonlinear interactions and feedback loops that create new constraints and change behavior.
options.C: Because rigid boundaries stop parts from reorganizing relationships.
options.D: Because outside factors alone determine the system's outputs.

questions.simplification_notes: Made the question two short sentences and used simple phrases. Kept the core idea that emergence comes from interactions, feedback, and changed constraints; distractors simplified but plausible.
concepts_tested: Emergence and holism: systems can be more than the sum of their parts, exhibiting synergy or emergent behavior.
Boundaries, context, and relations: causal/system boundaries are shaped by context and are expressed through the system’s structure, function, role, and relations with other systems.

Dynamics, learning/adaptation, and equifinality: systems model dynamics and constraints, adapt based on engagement with their environment, and may reach similar endpoints via multiple pathways (equifinality).

questions.question: A receptor senses a change and tells a control center to activate effectors. Why does effector activity fall as the variable returns to the set point, showing negative feedback?

options.A: Effectors keep pushing even near the set point, which is positive feedback.
options.B: The control center shuts down near the set point, which is feedforward control.
options.C: The effector’s stimulus falls as the variable returns, lowering output and stabilizing the system (negative feedback).
options.D: Receptors reset the set point, making a new baseline (adaptive feedback).

questions.simplification_notes: Made two short sentences, used simpler words, kept the receptor/control center/effector idea and the negative feedback explanation.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: The three-component feedback control system (receptor, control center, effector) and their interactions in maintaining a variable within a maintenance range.
Concept 2: Negative feedback as the mechanism that reverses changes and stabilizes the system once the variable returns to the set point.

Concept 3: Multi-variable regulation and cross-talk, where a single control center can regulate multiple variables (e.g., renin–angiotensin system) and signals propagate through receptors to effectors to maintain homeostasis.

questions.question: In a feedback loop, the error $e = SP - PV$ tells the controller how far the process is from the target. How does increasing the loop gain change how fast the error is fixed and the system's stability?

options.A: Controller ignores the error and uses PV only; increasing gain has no effect.
options.B: Controller makes a corrective output proportional to the error; higher gain speeds correction but can cause oscillations or instability.
options.C: Controller differentiates PV to predict future error and higher gain always stabilizes the system.
options.D: Controller clamps outputs to fixed levels to force SP = PV, so gain changes nothing about dynamics.

questions.simplification_notes: Split into two short sentences; used $e = SP - PV$ for the error; removed complex terms and kept the main idea about error-driven control and gain trade-offs.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Feedback control loop—how the setpoint (SP) and process variable (PV) produce an error signal that drives corrective action to regulate the system.
Concept 2: Stability and performance criteria—how delays, overshoot, steady-state error, and stability criteria influence controller design and system behavior.

Concept 3: Modeling and design tools—how block diagrams and transfer functions model input-output relationships, and how controllability/observability relate to the ability to control and estimate system states.

questions.question: Why must a feedback loop be studied as a whole instead of following just one straight cause-and-effect path?

options.A: Because outputs return as inputs, making cause-and-effect circular so a change can loop back and alter the start.
options.B: Because feedback always makes signals bigger, so only the strongest path matters.
options.C: Because feedback always improves a system, so fixing one part is enough.
options.D: Because feedback keeps effects inside one part, so global checks are not needed.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened sentences, removed technical jargon, kept the idea of circular causality and system-wide effects. Options made brief and plausible.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Feedback forms a loop that creates circular causality, requiring system-wide analysis rather than linear cause-and-effect reasoning.
Concept 2: Feedback acts as a regulatory mechanism by routing outputs back to inputs to control a process (e.g., water level via a float valve; speed via governors).
Concept 3: Feedback can modify system gain and behavior (e.g., boosting amplification through regeneration) but may also cause instability or undesirable effects (e.g., howl) if not managed.

questions.question: How can simple local rules between parts create system-wide behaviors we cannot predict from one part alone?

options.A: The whole is just the sum of each part's behavior; nothing new appears.
options.B: Local interactions create nonlinear feedback (small actions can cause big effects) and constraints that form new large-scale patterns.
options.C: A single central agent imposes the global state after all parts finish interacting.
options.D: Emergent patterns are just measurement errors and disappear with better observation.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened and clarified into one sentence. Kept key ideas: emergence from local interactions, nonlinear feedback, and contrasted with sum, central control, and measurement error.
concepts_tested: Emergence: collective, system-wide properties arise from local interactions among components and cannot be predicted from individual parts alone.
Self-organization and adaptation: order and patterns arise without central control through local rules and feedback, enabling systems to adapt to their environment.
Networked relationships and nonlinear dynamics: representing systems as networks (nodes/links) to explain how interactions shape dynamics, including nonlinear behaviors and feedback loops.

questions.question: How does a cybernetic feedback loop let a system adapt to change instead of repeat the same action?

options.A: It blocks all outside influence so the system never changes.
options.B: Outputs return as inputs, creating an error or state estimate the system uses to adjust its actions.
options.C: It records one past output and replays it each time, ignoring new changes.
options.D: It forces the environment to change to match the system through external control.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened sentences, simpler words, kept the core idea that outputs feed back as inputs and enable adjustment; examples and history removed.
concepts_tested: Circular causality and feedback loops as the mechanism by which a system's outputs become inputs that influence future actions.
Recursion and information processing as part of how cybernetic systems control and adapt to changing environments.
Transdisciplinary applicability: the idea that general cybernetic principles apply across engineering, biology, sociology, design, etc., and enable cross-domain communication.

questions.question: How does Peirce's three-part sign model change how signs make meaning? Peirce's parts are sign vehicle, object, and interpretant; Saussure's are signifier and signified.

options.A: Meaning is fixed by the sign vehicle's resemblance to the object.
options.B: Meaning comes only from social conventions linking signifier and signified, with no mental process.
options.C: It adds the interpretant, a mental effect that varies by interpreter and context, letting signs create new signs.
options.D: The interpreter's beliefs do not affect meaning; the sign alone determines it.

questions.simplification_notes: Made the question two short sentences, named each model's parts, used simple words. Options kept short and plausible; option C explains the interpretant as a variable mental effect allowing new meanings.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: The three types of signs (iconic, indexical, symbolic) and how their basis (similarity, direct physical link, convention) shapes interpretation.
Concept 2: Saussurean (dyadic) vs. Peirce (triadic) models of signs and what the different component structures imply about meaning-making.
Concept 3: Sign systems, texts, and codes (conventional vs. natural codes) and the role of context and tropes in determining message interpretation.

questions.question: Why are synchronic and diachronic studies useful together? How does using both improve explanations of language structure and change?

options.A: They both study a language at the same time with different methods to confirm universal rules.
options.B: Synchrony studies present structure; diachrony studies historical change. Together they show what stays stable and what changes.
options.C: They are mutually exclusive; you must pick one to avoid contradictory conclusions about language.
options.D: Synchrony studies mental grammar; diachrony studies social use. Using both wrongly mixes mind and society.

questions.simplification_notes: Question split into two short sentences. Technical terms kept but explained by contrast. Options shortened and kept plausible; correct choice unchanged.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: The distinction and relationship between theoretical linguistics (universal properties, framework development) and applied linguistics (practical language education and literacy).
Concept 2: Synchronically vs. diachronically as complementary perspectives for studying language structure and change.
Concept 3: Interdisciplinary relationships of linguistics with philology, philosophy of language, semiotics, lexicography, translation, and related fields.

questions.question: Why do social norms make people follow group rules even when some want to break them?

options.A: Because norms create social rewards and punishments and set others' expectations, making behavior predictable.
options.B: Because norms are equivalent to written laws enforced by authorities.
options.C: Because norms perfectly match everyone's personal preferences, so people always agree.
options.D: Because norms have no effect and people act randomly.

questions.simplification_notes: Question and options shortened and simplified; kept idea that norms work via social sanctions, expectations, and predictability; removed technical language and extra theory details.
concepts_tested: Social norms as mechanisms that regulate behavior and sustain social order
Division of labor and social roles as enabling cooperation and the emergence of complex social structures
Bidirectional relationship between individuals and society (mutual shaping and socialization)

questions.question: Why do mental set and functional fixedness stop people from finding a solution when the same problem is shown in a different way?

options.A: They make solvers try many ideas and lose focus on what matters.
options.B: They push solvers to use familiar methods and usual tool functions, blocking new representations.
options.C: They overload working memory so people cannot hold the steps of the problem.
options.D: They make people stick to simple rules or instincts even when those are wrong.

questions.simplification_notes: Wrote one short question sentence and made all options short and clear for middle school readers while keeping the original idea that these biases narrow thinking and block alternative views of the problem.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Well-defined vs ill-defined problems and the impact on planning and approach.
Concept 2: Cognitive obstacles to problem solving (e.g., confirmation bias, mental set, functional fixedness) and ways these impede finding solutions.

Concept 3: Problem solving as a domain-spanning, process-oriented activity (identify, analyze, solve) influenced by context (domains like mathematics vs personal problems) and resource/knowledge requirements.

questions.question: Why might a company buy or control its suppliers when it needs special parts or faces high deal costs?

options.A: To gain bigger economies of scale from the open market.
options.B: To cut total transaction costs like bargaining, enforcing contracts, and hold-up. Hold-up means one side can exploit the other after a special investment.
options.C: To follow rules or regulations that force them to own suppliers of special inputs.
options.D: To move information problems inside the firm so outside markets no longer matter.

questions.simplification_notes: Rewrote the question in simple words, used 'special parts' for asset specificity, shortened sentences, explained 'hold-up' briefly, and kept the main idea that internalizing reduces transaction costs.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: How transaction costs, imperfect information, and entry barriers shape the boundary between firms and markets and affect organizational structure.
Concept 2: How strategic interaction among firms (via game theory) explains oligopoly behavior, pricing strategies, and contract design.
Concept 3: How industrial organization theory informs public policy, antitrust regulation, and governance of law, linking theoretical models to policy outcomes.

questions.question: How does studying the factors that affect a group's health help design public health interventions?

options.A: It finds upstream, changeable causes (social, economic, environmental) to target and prevent disease.
options.B: It leads to focusing mainly on individual medical treatments, ignoring community causes.
options.C: It makes people think health is only fixed by biology, reducing public health action.
options.D: It causes random, low-cost actions that don't match the detected health causes.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the question and options, used simpler words, explained 'upstream' as changeable causes, kept the idea that analysis guides targeting root causes.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Determinants of health and population analysis guide public health actions (how understanding determinants leads to targeted interventions).
Concept 2: Public health mechanisms (surveillance, health promotion, vaccination, environmental health) are the causal processes by which health is protected and improved.
Concept 3: Public health as an interdisciplinary field that intersects with healthcare systems and policy, requiring integration across disciplines to address population health.

questions.question: How do social determinants of health affect people's health without medical care?

options.A: They change people's genes across generations.
options.B: They shape daily life—income, housing, education, and neighborhood safety. These cause chronic stress, harmful exposures, and less access to resources, harming health apart from medical care.
options.C: They only decide where clinics are, so they affect health just by care access.
options.D: They alter how dangerous germs are.

questions.simplification_notes: Made the question short and clear for middle school. Simplified vocabulary and split option B into two short sentences to show non-medical pathways (stress, exposures, resource access).
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Social determinants affect health outcomes through non-medical factors and mechanisms (e.g., chronic stress, access to resources, environmental conditions).
Concept 2: Life-course perspective: early-life social conditions influence health trajectories across the lifespan.
Concept 3: Policy and economic policy shape health equity by distributing resources and opportunities, producing health disparities.

questions.question: Why must national, regional, and local policies, funding, and rules match to scale up a health program? How do governance choices help or block its expansion?

options.A: Scaling up depends only on local willingness; national policies and funding do not matter.
options.B: Governance at all levels controls resources, regulations, and implementation rules; misalignment creates bottlenecks, while alignment enables predictable support for expansion.
options.C: Strong evidence alone does not guarantee scale-up if governance and funding are not aligned.
options.D: If national policy is adopted, local and regional levels will automatically implement it without coordination.

questions.simplification_notes: Used shorter sentences, removed technical jargon, and kept the core idea about multilevel governance, funding, and alignment affecting scale-up.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: The policy process and health systems governance — how policy development translates into programs and services, and how decisions at multiple levels enable or constrain health outcomes and scale-up.
Concept 2: Evidence-based policy — the role of science and rigorous studies (e.g., randomized controlled trials) in identifying programs and practices that improve policy-relevant outcomes.
Concept 3: Political and economic determinants of policy adoption — how debates about rights, ethics, government authority, efficiency, and costs shape which policies are pursued or implemented.

questions.question: How does morphogenesis form the three germ layers: ectoderm, mesoderm, and endoderm? Why can't tissue growth by cell proliferation do this alone?

options.A: It uses coordinated cell movements and tissue remodeling to rearrange cells into layers, while growth only increases size.
options.B: By just making the tissue bigger so different regions spontaneously become the three layers without any cell movement.
options.C: By regional specification alone from cytoplasmic determinants, so no cell rearrangement is needed.
options.D: By only inductive signals that set layer identities, without any mechanical or positional reorganization.

questions.simplification_notes: Split the original complex sentence into two short sentences, used simpler words, and kept each option concise and plausible.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Regional specification establishes spatial patterning through cytoplasmic determinants and inductive signals, guiding later differentiation.
Concept 2: Morphogenesis drives three-dimensional shaping via coordinated cell movements and tissue remodeling, enabling germ layer formation and organ development.
Concept 3: Tissue growth and allometry contribute to morphogenesis through proliferation, cell size changes, and extracellular matrix deposition, affecting overall shape and structure.

questions.question: Why is transcription initiation a main control point for gene expression? How do signals and the cell's context change when and how transcription starts?

options.A: Signals mainly affect steps after initiation, so initiation cannot be easily regulated.
options.B: Signals change transcription factor activity and chromatin at promoters and enhancers, so initiation depends on cell context.
options.C: Initiation is controlled only by DNA sequence motifs and not by signals or chromatin.
options.D: Initiation is controlled only by the amount of RNA polymerase, not by signaling.

questions.simplification_notes: Split the original question into two short sentences, used simpler words, and shortened each option while keeping the core ideas about transcription initiation, signaling, transcription factors, and chromatin.
concepts_tested: Regulation occurs at multiple stages, with transcription initiation being a central control point, influenced by signaling and cellular context.
Chromatin structure and epigenetic modifications (DNA methylation, histone modifications, chromatin remodeling) regulate gene accessibility and expression, with potential heritable effects.
Gene regulation forms networks and pathways that drive development, differentiation, and adaptive responses, linking regulatory cascades to organismal biology and evolution.

questions.question: Why do scientists need outside information, like an outgroup, to root a phylogenetic tree? How does the chosen root change whether a trait is called ancestral or derived?

options.A: The root is just decoration and does not change how we read the tree.
options.B: The root gives a time direction by showing the common ancestor. If the root is wrong, the inferred direction of change can be wrong.
options.C: Rooting reorders species at the tips and so changes the tree topology.
options.D: Rooting turns the tree into a network that shows horizontal gene transfer.

questions.simplification_notes: Made wording shorter and clearer for middle school. Split complex idea into two sentences. Kept focus on need for outgroup/root and effect on labeling traits ancestral vs derived.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Phylogenetic inference uses observable, heritable data to reconstruct evolutionary relationships and yields a phylogenetic tree as a diagram of inferred history.
Concept 2: Rooted vs unrooted trees convey different directional information about ancestry and character-state transformations.

Concept 3: The broad, cross-domain applications of phylogenetics (biodiversity, cancer clonal evolution, drug discovery, forensics) demonstrate the unifying concept of relatedness across organisms, genes, and cells.

questions.question: Laws can come from legislatures, the executive, and judges. Which choice best explains how this keeps order but allows correcting overreach?

options.A: The legislature always has final control, so executives and courts must follow it.
options.B: Higher rules like a constitution limit all sources; courts can strike down overreach and legislatures can change laws.
options.C: Courts alone resolve conflicts, so statutes and regulations depend on judges' choices.
options.D: There is no hierarchy; each source acts alone and conflicts are settled by politics.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened sentences, simpler words, and clearer examples of checks (courts striking down, legislatures revising) while keeping the original idea.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Sources and mechanisms of law creation and enforcement (legislation, executive actions, judicial decisions; how they interact and can override one another)
Concept 2: Public vs private law and cross-system variation (how the division shapes governance, disputes, and the operation of civil vs. common law systems)
Concept 3: Law as a mediator of social relations and its effects on politics, economics, and society (how legal rules influence and reflect social outcomes)

questions.question: Why do legal positivists say 'what the law is' can be studied apart from 'what it ought to be'? What rule or idea lets us separate facts about law from moral judgments?

options.A: Law's validity is set by socially recognized rules and sources, called the 'rule of recognition'. This lets descriptive study stay separate from moral evaluation.
options.B: Law's validity depends on moral rightness, so 'is' and 'ought' cannot be separated.
options.C: Law is grounded in divine commands, so what it is and ought to be are linked.
options.D: Only empirical outcomes decide what counts as law, making moral questions irrelevant.

questions.simplification_notes: Made sentences shorter and clearer for middle school level; defined the 'rule of recognition' as socially recognized rules; kept the positivist separation idea.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: The analytic vs normative jurisprudence distinction and the question of whether what law is must be connected to what it ought to be (legal positivism vs natural law theory).
Concept 2: Natural law as a basis for legitimacy of human laws, asserting rational, objective limits to power and that law's force derives from these laws of nature.

Concept 3: The social-scientific turn in jurisprudence (sociological and experimental) and the idea that law’s nature and function can be studied empirically across cultures and through empirical methods.

questions.question: How does random noise help a nonlinear system with lots of feedback find strong, stable patterns (attractors)?

options.A: Noise damps fluctuations and forces the system straight into the nearest attractor, blocking exploration.
options.B: Noise makes the system try many states, escape weak patterns, and feedback amplifies paths into a strong attractor.
options.C: Noise makes all parts follow the same random signal, creating uniform but fragile order.
options.D: Noise overwhelms feedback so the system stays random and never forms attractors.

questions.simplification_notes: Made the question one short sentence and explained 'attractors' as stable patterns; options are concise and keep original meanings.
concepts_tested: Emergence from local interactions in nonlinear, feedback-rich systems
The four ingredients of self-organization (nonlinearity with feedback, energy input, multiple interactions, balance of exploitation and exploration)
The role of noise/random perturbations in enabling exploration of state space and movement toward robust attractors (order from noise)

questions.question: How does clustering homes, jobs, and shops near a transit hub change a city's layout and everyday life?

options.A: The dense node creates agglomeration economies (benefits when people and firms cluster), lowers travel costs, attracts firms and residents, and increases daily social interactions.
options.B: The node automatically spreads growth evenly across the whole city without any policy help.
options.C: Density near transit has no effect on where people live or work; choices depend only on personal preference.
options.D: Higher density near transit always lowers accessibility because of congestion, making the area less attractive.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the question, used simpler words, explained 'agglomeration economies', and kept the core idea about clustering near transit drawing people and firms.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Spatial structures as the physical organization of a city and their influence on social/economic outcomes.
Concept 2: Processes that support spatial structure (the mechanisms by which city form operates and affects urban life).
Concept 3: Normative analysis in urban studies (using evidence to advocate for better planning and policy).

questions.question: In ideas like Foucault's Panopticon, how can a building make people follow rules without guards?

options.A: Put written laws on the walls that people must obey.
options.B: Make actions visible so people watch themselves and behave correctly.
options.C: Use machines or tech to force correct actions even if no one sees them.
options.D: Keep groups apart and give each group fixed roles and limits.

questions.simplification_notes: Rewrote the question in one simple sentence. Replaced technical phrasing with 'make people follow rules without guards.' Shortened options and kept them plausible.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Architecture as part of philosophy of art, dealing with aesthetic value, semantics, and its relationship to culture.
Concept 2: Architecture as a social/disciplinary instrument that can shape society and behavior (e.g., Foucault’s Panopticon and related ideas).
Concept 3: The modernization shift where technology and mechanization redefine architectural aesthetics (engineering aesthetics, Constructivism, Functionalism, Cubism/Futurism).

questions.question: Why can techne (craft) finish what nature (physis) cannot, and how does its external telos (an outside goal) help it?

options.A: Techne uses inner natural goals and lets processes finish themselves without human direction.
options.B: Techne copies nature exactly and is limited by nature's ends, so it cannot go beyond them.
options.C: Techne is guided by an external telos set by human purposes, so it directs and completes what nature leaves unfinished.
options.D: Techne and physis share the same inner ends and being, so both finish natural processes the same way.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened sentences, defined telos as an outside goal, kept Aristotle's distinction between techne and physis and the role of external ends.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Techne as imitation of nature and its potential to complete what nature cannot finish; the ontological distinction between techne and physis.
Concept 2: The role of telos (final cause) in technology, with techne being shaped by external causes and ends, unlike natural processes that have an internal end.

Concept 3: Historical-philosophical frameworks linking technology to social power and governance (e.g., Bacon’s New Atlantis and the notion of expanding human empire through knowledge of causes), as well as later figures like Ernst Kapp.

questions.question: How does modernist art draw attention to how it was made, and why does that change what the artwork means?

options.A: It shows the methods and materials, so meaning comes from making as well as the finished work.
options.B: It hides how it was made to keep the myth of a single genius and pure meaning.
options.C: It claims meaning is fixed after completion and does not depend on the making.
options.D: It says technical perfection alone makes meaning universal, no matter the process.

questions.simplification_notes: Made the sentence shorter and clearer for middle school readers; kept the idea that modernists highlight process and that process shapes meaning.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Experimentalism and rejection of tradition as the defining method of modernist art, including moving away from absolute originality and using techniques like collage, parody, and rewriting.
Concept 2: Reflexivity about artistic process and materiality, i.e., art being self-referential and focused on how works are made as part of the work itself.

Concept 3: Contextual relationships and drivers, such as Modernism arising from secularization, science, industrialization, urbanization, and post–World War I shifts, and its critique of Enlightenment rationalism through these connections.

questions.question: Why does more usable energy per person help a society grow and develop?

options.A: Because more energy alone makes new tools without changes in knowledge or organization.
options.B: Because more energy lets societies make more goods, build surpluses, let people specialize, and drive new inventions.
options.C: Because energy per person determines a society's morals and politics, which alone cause development.
options.D: Because more energy removes the need for human labor and makes organization unnecessary.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened and simplified wording to middle school level; kept the mechanism that extra energy enables greater production, surpluses, job specialization, and innovation. Correct answer unchanged.
concepts_tested: The co-evolution of technology, science, and society
Energy harnessing as a driver of cultural and economic development
Information as a central driver of societal advancement

questions.question: When a sensor detects a variable is off from its set point, effectors (parts that act) respond. Why does negative feedback keep things stable as deviations get larger?

options.A: It makes the change bigger by activating effectors that push the variable the same way as the error.
options.B: It acts in the opposite direction; bigger errors make a stronger corrective response that moves the variable back.
options.C: It ignores small changes to save energy and only reacts to large disturbances, which can reduce oscillations.
options.D: It resets the set point permanently after a disturbance, so the new baseline is different.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened sentences, explained 'effectors' in parentheses, used simpler words while keeping the idea of negative feedback and scaling of response.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Homeostasis and regulatory mechanisms maintain stable internal conditions through feedback and control processes.
Concept 2: Structure-function relationship—the anatomy and organization of organisms influence and constrain physiological function.

Concept 3: Multilevel integration and intercellular communication—physiological processes are coordinated across molecules, cells, organs, and populations via biophysical/biochemical processes and signaling.

questions.question: Why can one text have many sensible interpretations but no single "true" meaning?

options.A: Different theories focus on language, culture, or power and so highlight different meaningful features.
options.B: There is one fixed meaning, and theories only hide or show it.
options.C: Interpretations come only from readers' feelings, not from any theory.
options.D: All meanings already sit in the plot; theories just pull them out randomly.

questions.simplification_notes: Made the question shorter and clearer for middle school readers, removed technical jargon, turned the original multi-clause sentence into a single direct question. Options kept concise and plausible.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Meaning in literature is not fixed; interpretation is mediated by theoretical frameworks, allowing multiple readings.
Concept 2: Literary theory comprises diverse approaches (semiotics, philosophy of language, cultural studies, continental philosophy) that shape how we read and interpret texts.

Concept 3: The development of theory is tied to broader intellectual and cultural movements, including debates over aims (close reading vs. ideology/culture critique), showing cause-and-effect in methodological shifts.

questions.question: Why does mixing methods and assumptions from different fields solve problems better than just adding each field's ideas?

options.A: It gives more data, so answers are always better.
options.B: One field leads and decides the problem, cutting confusion.
options.C: It uncovers and fixes different assumptions and methods. This builds a true mix that uses strengths and closes blind spots.
options.D: It removes the need for team talk by forcing a single method for everyone.

questions.simplification_notes: Made the question one clear sentence and used simpler words. Shortened options to 1–2 short sentences while keeping the core idea about reconciling assumptions and methods.
concepts_tested: Integration of multiple disciplines to address complex, real-world problems through synthesis of perspectives and methods.
Educational mechanisms (team-taught courses, connecting/combining approaches) that develop cognitive flexibility, active learning, critical thinking, and problem-solving.
Structural/relational aspect: crossing traditional boundaries to form interdisciplinary fields or organizational units and to address subjects neglected within traditional disciplines.

questions.question: Why do discourse analysts change sentences into a 'canonical form'? How does that help show how meaning is arranged across the whole text?

options.A: They merge all sentences into one unit, making the meaning fixed and easy to compare.
options.B: They change sentences so equivalent information appears in the same positions, letting analysts compare across sentences.
options.C: They remove style differences and keep only word counts, assuming meaning comes from word frequency alone.
options.D: They shuffle sentence order randomly to see if the order matters for understanding.

questions.simplification_notes: Split the original complex question into two short sentences. Used simple words and shorter phrases. Kept the idea of sentence transformations aligning equivalent information across sentences.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Discourse analysis aims to reveal socio-psychological characteristics by analyzing language beyond sentence boundaries.
Concept 2: It prioritizes naturally occurring (real-life) language use over invented examples, highlighting a methodological principle about data selection.
Concept 3: The analysis of informational content and structural relations (e.g., sublanguage analysis, transformations to canonical form) as mechanisms to uncover how discourse organizes meaning.

questions.question: Why do debates about which human rights come first change how countries write laws and policies?

options.A: Because universal rights must be enforced everywhere at once, leaving no room for local change.
options.B: Because local cultures shape which rights seem most important. Countries often protect some rights first, while international rules set goals for slow, practical change.
options.C: Because every society already agrees on the order of rights, so debates don't affect policy.
options.D: Because money always solves cultural conflicts, so arguments about which rights matter become unimportant.

questions.simplification_notes: Made the question shorter and clearer for middle school readers, used simple vocabulary, split the correct option into two short sentences to explain cultural influence and gradual international guidance, and kept all answers plausible.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Universal and inalienable nature of human rights as moral principles protected by law
Concept 2: The Universal Declaration of Human Rights as a catalytic framework that inspires international treaties and national laws
Concept 3: Tension between universal rights and cultural relativism, and how debates about precedence and implementation influence practice

questions.question: In a federation, the constitution gives some powers to the central government, some to states, and some to both. Why does this cause separate court systems and need a higher court to settle disputes?

options.A: It says only the central court may decide constitutional disputes, so all state disputes must go there.
options.B: It creates separate court systems for each level and a supreme court for conflicts in shared areas.
options.C: It forces state courts not to interpret any national laws.
options.D: It lets each legislature veto the other level's judicial decisions.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened sentences, simpler words, and clearer phrasing while keeping federalism, separate courts, and a higher court to resolve conflicts.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Separation of powers and checks/balances—how the constitution delineates and constrains the roles and powers of the executive, legislature, and judiciary, and how hierarchies or balances of power are established.

Concept 2: Federalism vs. unitary governance and jurisdictional relationships—how constitutions allocate powers across central and subnational authorities and define exclusive or shared domains, including how this affects court structures.

Concept 3: Codified vs. uncodified constitutional frameworks and mechanisms—how constitutions rely on written texts, conventions, statutes, customary law, and other rules, and how their status and operation influence rights protection and governance.
