options.A: Structures decide everything; societies stay the same because of fixed rules, and change only comes from big outside shocks.
options.B: Agency works alone; stability is an illusion and change comes only from individuals acting freely, not social limits.
options.C: Structures give patterns, resources, and norms that guide and limit actions. Over time people reproduce or reinterpret these practices, keeping stability or causing change.
options.D: Social systems are just random interactions, so neither structure nor agency explains stability or change.

questions.simplification_notes: Defined 'structure' and 'agency', split into short clear sentences, and kept the core idea that structures guide actions while agents reproduce or transform them.
concepts_tested: Ethically grounded interpretation of society (using ethics/values as the lens for analyzing social institutions)
Structure and agency as a central relational framework in social phenomena
Interdisciplinary relationships and the development of theoretical frameworks (e.g., cross-links with ethics, political philosophy, sociology, and frameworks like social ontology, care ethics)

questions.question: Why does changing the mode of production (how society makes goods) mark a big turning point in history?

options.A: Because it first changes people's beliefs and values.
options.B: Because it reshapes who owns and controls production, creating new classes and class struggles.
options.C: Because it is driven mainly by noble ideas and political slogans.
options.D: Because it happens mainly from random external shocks that topple regimes.

questions.simplification_notes: Made the question shorter and added a short parenthetical explanation of 'mode of production'. Options are concise and keep the original meanings.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: The mode of production as the core driver of historical change, with economic structure shaping history and producing class relations.
Concept 2: Technological development as a catalyst that transforms the mode of production and, in turn, social organization.
Concept 3: Class division and class struggle as the central dynamics through which changes in production and economy produce historical change.

questions.question: In the base–superstructure view, why do big changes in the economic base usually cause changes in laws, government, and ideas? The "economic base" means how goods are produced and who controls production.

options.A: The superstructure (laws and ideas) decides how production works.
options.B: Material production relations set incentives and limits, so new production methods force laws, politics, and ideas to change.
options.C: Economic growth alone automatically changes culture and ideas, regardless of political systems.
options.D: The base and superstructure are completely separate; one change does not affect the other.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the question to two clear sentences, added a brief definition of "economic base," and made all four answer choices short and plausible while keeping answer B as correct.
concepts_tested: Historical materialism/dialectical materialism as the framework for historical development based on material conditions and conflict
Base–superstructure relationship: the economic base shaping political, legal, ideological, and cultural life
Class exploitation and struggle: the bourgeoisie–proletariat dynamics leading to crises, alienation, and the potential transition to socialism/communism

questions.question: How does the 'Pictorial Turn' change how we know and understand the world? What causes this change?

options.A: Says language stays primary and images only illustrate, not change meaning.
options.B: Sees images as primary mediators that shape evidence and interpretation through visual technologies and rapid spread.
options.C: Says all reasoning is replaced by guessing from perception, ending structured critique.
options.D: Argues visual culture has no effect and only mirrors language categories.

questions.simplification_notes: Split the complex question into two short sentences, removed technical terms, and focused on the idea that images shape knowledge and that visual technology and fast dissemination drive the shift.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Mediation of perception and meaning through visual technology and images (how visuals structure experience and information).
Concept 2: Pictorial Turn (and iconic turn) — a paradigm shift where images become primary in shaping our relation to the world, analogous to the linguistic turn.
Concept 3: Interdisciplinary and multimedia scope — visual culture overlaps with film studies, cognitive science, technology, and various media, shaping its theoretical frameworks and methods.

questions.question: Why does saying facts about a sample not prove the same facts about the whole population? How does a statistical model help connect the sample to the population?

options.A: Descriptive stats only summarize observed data and do not show uncertainty about unobserved people. A statistical model says how sampling and the population make the data, and it predicts other samples and measures uncertainty.
options.B: Samples always perfectly match the population, so models are unnecessary.
options.C: A sample is just a fixed part of the population, so you could recalculate statistics for the whole group.
options.D: A model is needed because it will guarantee each sample gives identical estimates of the population.

questions.simplification_notes: Question shortened to two clear sentences; technical wording simplified; answer choices made short and plausible; kept core idea that models show data generation and quantify uncertainty.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: The central role of selecting a statistical model of the data-generating process and translating a real-world problem into a statistical model as the core of inference.
Concept 2: The inference-to-population relationship, highlighting that conclusions about a population rely on sampling and differ from descriptive statistics that focus only on observed data.

Concept 3: The different forms of inferential propositions (point estimates, interval estimates such as confidence or credible intervals, hypothesis testing, clustering/classification) and what they imply about parameters or data structure.

questions.question: Why do wide, shared research networks make more long-term innovation than isolated, local teams?

options.A: Because they concentrate money and ideas in a few top institutions, speeding breakthroughs there.
options.B: Because they bring diverse problem views and speed tacit knowledge through repeated teamwork. They also let groups share rare equipment, lower costs, and avoid duplicated work.
options.C: Because siloed labs protect secret knowledge and so increase private gains.
options.D: Because cross-border collaboration can weaken national control and should be avoided to keep domestic expertise.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened and clarified wording to middle-school level. Kept key ideas: diverse problem framing, tacit knowledge transfer, shared equipment, lower costs, and reduced duplication. Options made short and plausible.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Resource allocation aligned with the public interest to maximize societal benefits, and its impact on science outcomes.
Concept 2: Knowledge networks, collaborations, and the distribution of expertise/equipment as mechanisms shaping science policy and innovation.
Concept 3: Multi-actor governance and internationalization of science policy, including the roles of government, private sector, NGOs, universities, and cross-border collaboration.

questions.question: Why does science use testable predictions and falsifiability (being able to prove an idea wrong)? How does that make scientific ideas change over time?

options.A: Experiments can disprove ideas, so failed predictions force revision or replacement, improving understanding.
options.B: It ensures that once a hypothesis is proven, it cannot be challenged again, guaranteeing truth.
options.C: It makes science depend on the authority of established theories rather than experimental evidence.
options.D: It removes the need for replication because one correct prediction proves the whole theory.

questions.simplification_notes: Question shortened into two clear sentences; 'falsifiability' briefly defined; options made short and age-appropriate.
concepts_tested: The scientific method as a systematic approach: testable hypotheses and predictions guiding knowledge about the universe.
The relationships and distinct methodologies among branches: natural, social, formal, and applied sciences and how their methods differ (empirical/scientific method vs deductive reasoning).
The historical development of science: transition from natural philosophy to natural science during the Scientific Revolution and its impact on how knowledge is acquired and validated.

questions.question: Why do persistent identifiers (e.g., DOI) and standardized metadata help researchers repeat studies?

options.A: They guarantee the dataset has no errors and is identical every time.
options.B: They give a stable, citable link and clear machine-readable details to find, understand, and reuse the exact data.
options.C: They cause data to be automatically peer-reviewed before release.
options.D: They remove the need to record how data were collected.

questions.simplification_notes: Made one short question sentence and simplified technical phrasing. Options kept short and plausible. Core idea that identifiers and metadata enable finding, interpreting, and reusing exact data was preserved.
concepts_tested: Concept 1
Concept 2
Concept 3

questions.question: Why do scientists use global events like mass extinctions to mark the start of geologic time units? Why not use rocks from a single region?

options.A: Because global events are time-equivalent everywhere, letting scientists correlate rocks worldwide.
options.B: Because rocks from one region, if well studied, can define boundaries alone.
options.C: Because extinction events are always dated exactly by radiometric methods.
options.D: Because boundaries are set by arbitrary numeric ages, not by natural events.

questions.simplification_notes: Split into two short sentences, used simpler words, kept core idea that global events give time-equivalent markers for worldwide correlation; answers made concise and plausible.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Chronostratigraphy and geochronology together tie rock strata to time to define divisions of geological history.
Concept 2: The law of superposition (and other stratigraphic principles) establish the relative ordering of rocks, informing chronostratigraphic positions.
Concept 3: International standardization (ICS/IUGS/ICC) creates globally consistent boundaries and units, often using major events (e.g., extinction events) as markers.

questions.question: How does emergence challenge strict reductionism? Why can new higher-level features appear that are not just the sum of parts, even when we know all parts and interactions?

options.A: The system loses all trace of the parts, so reduction is impossible.
options.B: They come from organized interactions of parts. This makes new macro-properties not obvious from parts alone, though they follow the same micro-laws.
options.C: Macro features are just averaged micro properties, so reduction is straightforward.
options.D: Macro properties are completely independent of micro interactions and need new, separate laws.

questions.simplification_notes: Split the original complex sentence into two short questions. Used simpler words and two short sentences per complex option. Kept the idea that emergence makes new properties appear from interactions and that these still follow micro-laws.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: The three-fold typology of reductionism (ontological, methodological, theory reductionism) and how each differs in scope and aim.
Concept 2: The relationship between reductionism and emergence (how higher-level features can arise from parts and may not be reducible to simple sums).

Concept 3: Mechanistic explanations and practical forms of reduction (examples like reducing gas temperature to molecular kinetic energy; psychophysical and physico-chemical reductionism; translation/derivation/explanation within theory reduction).

questions.question: Why is active surveillance used during disease outbreaks? How does it trade off timeliness, completeness, and resource use?

options.A: It finds cases proactively, making detection faster and more complete. It needs many more resources, so it suits outbreaks where missed cases can keep spread going.
options.B: It uses voluntary reports from facilities, so it is cheaper and faster. Therefore it is rarely used in outbreaks.
options.C: It removes the need for data checks, so it uses fewer resources. It gives faster results no matter the situation.
options.D: It surveys every person and finds all cases. This removes any trade-offs with resource use.

questions.simplification_notes: Split into two short sentences; used simpler words and shorter option sentences while keeping the original trade-off idea that active surveillance is more timely and complete but costlier.
concepts_tested: The surveillance cycle: continuous data collection, analysis, and interpretation used for planning, implementation, and evaluation of public health practice.
Passive vs. active surveillance: different mechanisms with trade-offs (reporting burden, completeness, timeliness) and appropriate use cases (e.g., active surveillance for epidemics).
Integration of data systems and informatics: how registries, automated reporting, and medical/public health informatics relate to surveillance effectiveness and health governance.

questions.question: Why does the four-principle approach in healthcare not use a fixed ranking for autonomy, non-maleficence, beneficence, and justice? How should conflicts between them be handled?

options.A: It sets a fixed hierarchy, so the top principle always wins.
options.B: They are prima facie duties that can conflict. Professionals must weigh reasons, justify choices, and try to honor several principles.
options.C: It judges only by outcomes, so you pick the action that gives the best overall result.
options.D: It follows only legal rules, so moral principles are optional if they clash with law.

questions.simplification_notes: Made sentences short and clear for middle school; kept technical terms like 'prima facie' but explained their role; shortened options and preserved the core idea that principles are weighed case by case.
concepts_tested: The four-principle framework (autonomy, non-maleficence, beneficence, justice) as a decision-making tool in health care and bioethics.
The relationship between applied ethics and normative/meta-ethics, including how applied ethics draws on consequentialist and deontological approaches.
The interdisciplinary, real-world scope of applied ethics (multi-professional context, linkage to fields like medicine, business, information technology, and the development of ethical codes).

questions.question: Why must broad moral ideas about fairness and well-being become clear rules for health policy? Stakeholders are the people affected and the experts who help make decisions.

options.A: Because big ethical ideas already give exact actions, so no translation is needed.
options.B: Because to use ethics in real systems we must make values into clear, measurable rules. This allows fair, consistent, and accountable choices across cases and resource limits.
options.C: Because ethics should stay separate from policy, leaving decisions to technical experts.
options.D: Because ethics only matters for individual patients and cannot guide public health.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened wording to two simple sentences, explained 'stakeholders', split complex ideas into clear steps, and preserved the need to turn values into rules and processes.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Interdisciplinary relationships how biology, medicine, politics, law, theology, and philosophy interact within bioethics to analyze moral questions.

Concept 2: Scope and boundaries of bioethics debates about what questions fall under its purview (all biology/medicine vs. a subset, including “the ethics of the ordinary” and broader moral assessment).
Concept 3: From moral discernment to policy and practice how ethical judgments translate into medical policy, public health decisions, and broader social issues (environment, well-being).

questions.question: Why is it helpful to separate normative ethics, applied ethics, and metaethics when judging moral issues?

options.A: Each field asks a different question. Normative asks which general rules to follow; applied tests those rules in real cases; meta asks what moral claims mean, whether they are true, and how they motivate action.
options.B: Because all three try to answer the same "what should I do" question using different methods.
options.C: Because only normative ethics can decide what is morally permitted; applied ethics is merely descriptive.
options.D: Because metaethics provides practical rules to follow in daily life.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the question and options to one or two clear sentences. Kept the core idea: normative = rules, applied = real cases, meta = meaning/status/motivation.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: The division of ethics into normative ethics, applied ethics, and metaethics, and how each aims to address different questions about moral evaluation.
Concept 2: The major normative theories (consequentialism, deontology, virtue ethics) and the criteria they use to judge actions as morally right.
Concept 3: Metaethical questions about whether moral facts are objective, how moral knowledge is possible, and how moral judgments motivate behavior.

questions.question: Encapsulation hides a module's internal details. How does that help keep software easy to maintain as it changes over time?

options.A: It guarantees better performance when internals change because those details are hidden.
options.B: It keeps changes inside a module and shows only stable interfaces. This stops changes from spreading elsewhere.
options.C: It forces you to edit every module that depends on it when you change it.
options.D: It removes the need for interfaces, letting implementation change without any contracts.

questions.simplification_notes: Made the question two short sentences and explained encapsulation briefly. Answer choices were shortened and kept plausible while preserving the original meaning.
concepts_tested: Concept 1
Concept 2
Concept 3

questions.question: Computer science asks what can and cannot be automated. Why does this show some tasks are impossible to automate?

options.A: Because every problem can be solved by a finite algorithm if we had enough time and memory.
options.B: Because human judgments are probabilistic and can't be described by a fixed algorithm.
options.C: Because some problems are undecidable or non-computable, so no algorithm solves them for every input.
options.D: Because hardware limits stop programs from fully handling messy real-world data.

questions.simplification_notes: Made the question one short sentence, used simpler words, briefly explained 'undecidable' in option C, kept four plausible answers.
concepts_tested: The fundamental aim of computer science: determining what can and cannot be automated (and the limits of automation).

The theory–practice relationship: computer science spans theoretical disciplines (algorithms, theory of computation, information theory) and applied disciplines (hardware/software design), illustrating how abstract models connect to real systems.

Artificial intelligence as goal-oriented problem solving: AI seeks to emulate goal-directed processes (problem-solving, planning, learning) with subfields like computer vision and natural language processing that apply these ideas to specific data types.

questions.question: Why does the label 'world music' group many different cultural styles? What effect can this have on listeners and musicians?

options.A: It groups diverse traditions under one marketing label, hiding differences and making music more alike.
options.B: It gives a precise scientific list of features that perfectly keeps each tradition separate.
options.C: It completely stops cross-cultural mixing by keeping 'world music' isolated from all other styles.
options.D: It comes only from artists calling themselves 'world music' and ignores industry or audience influence.

questions.simplification_notes: Split into two short sentences, used simpler vocabulary, kept the idea that industry grouping can hide differences and cause homogenization.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: World music as a marketing/classificatory construct that shapes how cultures and styles are perceived and bounded.
Concept 2: Globalization and technology (recording, travel, communication) as mechanisms that enable cross-cultural exchange and the emergence of crossover music.
Concept 3: Tension between preserving indigenous/local music-making and the homogenizing pressures of commercialization and global markets.

questions.question: Task A and Task B happen at the same time. Why does paying more attention to Task A make Task B worse?

options.A: Because attention is limited; using more for Task A leaves fewer resources for Task B.
options.B: Because practice makes attention unlimited, so Task B would not be affected.
options.C: Because Task A and Task B use completely separate brain channels, so they do not interfere.
options.D: Because the brain isolates tasks completely once one starts, preventing any trade-offs.

questions.simplification_notes: Split into two short sentences, used simple words, removed jargon, and kept the idea that attention is a limited resource and that focusing on one task reduces capacity for others.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Attention as a finite cognitive resource with limited processing capacity; allocating attention to one task reduces capacity for others.
Concept 2: Economic value and modeling of attention; treating attention as a scarce commodity and applying economic theory to information management and "free" goods.

Concept 3: Filtering to manage information overload; the need to filter out unimportant information rather than simply increasing information, and how systems design should reflect attention as the bottleneck that guides action.

questions.question: Why can one big platform's decision cause problems for many industries that depend on it?

options.A: Because the platform's modular design lets firms adapt without affecting others.
options.B: Because many industries rely on the platform's shared infrastructure: APIs, data, and rules. Changes can cut access and shift value, causing ripple effects across industries.
options.C: Because platform developers always optimize for long-term ecosystem health, so changes do not hurt others.
options.D: Because risks stay inside the platform's own revenue, so dependent industries remain safe.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened wording to one sentence question. Replaced technical terms with 'big platform' and 'shared infrastructure'. Explained cascading as 'ripple effects'.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Platform as infrastructural foundation enabling third-party activity and value creation
Concept 2: Dependency and systemic risk: industries rely on platform decisions, which can cascade and cause collapse
Concept 3: Governance/organizational models and relationships: platform capitalism vs. platform cooperativism vs. commons-based peer production (fairness, labor, and ownership dynamics)

questions.question: Why does flexible, goal-directed control need a network of brain areas instead of just one area?

options.A: One brain area would have to do many different tasks across many situations.
options.B: Different parts (frontal, parietal, subcortical) work together, allowing parallel processing and flexible integration.
options.C: Only subcortical areas do inhibitory control, so cortical areas are not needed for goals.
options.D: The posterior sensory cortex alone can form and keep goal-directed plans without frontal help.

questions.simplification_notes: Question shortened and simplified to one sentence. Technical idea of ‘distributed networks’ rephrased as ‘network of brain areas’. Options made brief and age-appropriate while keeping original meanings.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Hierarchical organization of executive functions (basic processes enabling higher-order skills like planning and problem-solving)
Concept 2: Distributed neural basis of executive functions (involvement of multiple brain regions, not solely the prefrontal cortex)
Concept 3: Competition between cognitive control and stimulus-driven behavior (inhibitory control as the mechanism for overriding stimulus-driven responses)

questions.question: Why is measuring only how someone feels right now a poor way to judge their overall happiness for life?

options.A: Because feelings now are stable and do not change with life events.
options.B: Because life-as-a-whole judgment equals current feeling, so measuring both seems redundant.
options.C: Because happiness includes both momentary feelings and whole-life judgments. These can differ because of memory bias, adaptation, and different evaluations.
options.D: Because only current feelings predict future health, while life satisfaction does not.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened wording, split complex ideas into two sentences, kept the key distinction between present experience and life-as-a-whole and reasons (memory bias, adaptation, differing evaluations).
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Happiness as a multidimensional construct encompassing current affect (emotions) and evaluative life satisfaction, with different frameworks (e.g., Kahneman’s focus on present experience vs. Veenhoven’s life-as-a-whole appraisal).

Concept 2: The relationship between happiness and broader outcomes (well-being, physical/mental health, social relationships, resilience), including measurement via subjective well-being and potential directionality issues.

Concept 3: Distinction between hedonic happiness (pleasure, short-term feelings) and eudaimonia (meaning, purpose, flourishing), and how philosophical/cultural usage influences definitions and interpretation.

questions.question: Why would a dominant firm make customers buy one product with another, and how does that hurt competition?

options.A: It moves buyers to the tied product and always improves welfare, letting new firms enter.
options.B: It signals perfect information and reduces uncertainty for rivals.
options.C: It uses power in one product to limit rivals in the tied product, blocking competition and raising entry barriers.
options.D: It lowers the firm's production costs, making it easier for rivals to match its prices.

questions.simplification_notes: Question shortened and 'tying' explained by saying 'make customers buy one product with another'; language simplified and sentences shortened.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Prohibition of agreements or practices that restrict free trading and competition (anti-cartel rules) and why cartels harm markets.
Concept 2: Prohibition of abusive behavior by a firm dominating a market (predatory pricing, tying, price gouging, refusal to deal) and why limiting such conduct protects competitive dynamics.
Concept 3: Merger and acquisition supervision, including potential remedies (divestiture, licensing, access requirements) to maintain competitive processes after consolidation.

questions.question: Why can a group's behavior be different from what you'd predict by studying each member alone?

options.A: Because each person's actions just add up to make the group's result.
options.B: Because members' interactions create new group behaviors and shared ways of thinking.
options.C: Because the leader alone decides everything for the group.
options.D: Because outside forces always control what the group does.

questions.simplification_notes: Replaced technical terms like 'emergent properties', 'nonlinear dynamics', and 'shared representations' with simpler phrases: 'new behaviors', 'interactions', and 'shared ways of thinking'. Shortened sentences and kept core idea that interactions produce group-level properties.
concepts_tested: Emergent properties of groups: the idea that group behavior cannot be fully understood by studying individuals in isolation.
Positive and negative group forces: mechanisms that drive decision-making, leadership, conformity, and intergroup interactions within groups.
Evolutionary mechanisms in group dynamics: social behaviors such as status, reciprocity, ostracism, and altruism as adaptations shaping group processes.

questions.question: A bureaucracy uses formal rules and a clear hierarchy to guide how people act. What benefit and trade-off does this create?

options.A: Relying on informal networks helps adaptability but lowers consistency.
options.B: Personal charisma motivates staff and boosts initiative and speed.
options.C: Formal rules, standard procedures, and hierarchy cut ambiguity and raise efficiency, but reduce flexibility and initiative.
options.D: Giving decision rights to everyone increases creativity and adaptability but hurts uniformity.

questions.simplification_notes: Split into two short sentences, used simpler words, kept bureaucracy idea and the efficiency vs flexibility trade-off.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Multi-level analysis in organizational behavior (micro, meso, macro) and their interactions
Concept 2: Role-based behavior — how individuals’ behavior changes when acting within organizational roles and under role expectations
Concept 3: Bureaucratic/rational-legal organizational design as a mechanism shaping behavior and organizational efficiency

questions.question: Abductive reasoning (making best guesses from clues) and non-verbal models (sketches, prototypes) are used in design thinking. Why does using both help teams solve ill-defined 'wicked' problems?

options.A: It guarantees one perfect solution by deductive logic from complete data.
options.B: Sketches and prototypes make hidden assumptions visible. Teams can quickly test many ideas, compare perspectives, and refine solutions.
options.C: The combination forces only numbers-based analysis and removes ambiguity.
options.D: Abductive reasoning removes user input and lets experts decide final designs.

questions.simplification_notes: Explained technical terms, split into two short sentences, kept the idea that externalizing and testing many ideas helps tackle wicked problems.
concepts_tested: Iterative, non-linear design process: context analysis, user testing, problem finding/framing, ideation, prototyping, evaluation, and how iteration supports problem solving.
Wicked problems and design thinking: features of ill-defined problems, multiple perspectives, lack of definitive formulations, and how design thinking accommodates ambiguity and stakeholder input.
Mechanisms of design thinking: abductive reasoning and non-verbal modelling (sketching, prototyping) as tools that enable sensemaking and creative solution generation.

questions.question: How does natural law limit or judge human-made (positive) laws?

options.A: It bases laws on universal moral principles found by reason, so laws that contradict them lose moral authority.
options.B: It says laws are only social contracts and cannot be judged by moral principles.
options.C: It claims positive law must follow the majority's will, regardless of moral standards.
options.D: It holds morality is private and subjective, so it offers no standard to judge laws.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened wording, used one clear question sentence and brief option sentences; removed historical detail while keeping the link that natural law grounds moral limits on human-made law.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Natural law consists of inherent, universal moral principles that are discoverable through reason and ground fundamental human rights.
Concept 2: Natural law serves as a normative mechanism that underlies, informs, or constrains positive (human-made) law, creating relationships between morality and legal systems.

Concept 3: The historical-development pathway of natural law (Aquinas' synthesis, Enlightenment influence, relation to natural rights and social contract) and its role in challenging divine right and shaping political-legal theory.

questions.question: A negative externality means producers ignore some costs they cause to others. How does a Pigouvian tax move the market toward Pareto efficiency?

options.A: It raises the producer's private marginal cost by the marginal external cost. This makes private marginal cost equal social marginal cost and aligns output.
options.B: It shifts the demand curve downward, reducing quantity to the efficient level.
options.C: It directly compensates harmed third parties, internalizing the harm.
options.D: It guarantees the externality is eliminated regardless of information or enforcement constraints.

questions.simplification_notes: Defined 'negative externality' in one sentence, split complex explanation into short sentences, and kept original economic idea that the tax raises private marginal cost to match social cost.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: The divergence between social costs/benefits and private costs/benefits due to externalities, and how this leads to market failure and potential Pareto inefficiency.
Concept 2: Internalization mechanisms (e.g., Pigouvian taxes) as a means to align private incentives with social costs/benefits, aiming for a Pareto-optimal outcome.
Concept 3: Distinction between negative and positive externalities and the practical challenges of policy design (such as informational limitations) in effectively internalizing them.

questions.question: How does deferring (waiting to start) an investment change a project's value when the future is uncertain?

options.A: It guarantees higher returns and stops any possible losses.
options.B: Managers can wait for new information before investing, making timing a choice and often increasing value through flexibility.
options.C: It fixes the project size now so the company avoids later expansion costs.
options.D: It turns the project into a tradable financial option on the market.

questions.simplification_notes: Rewrote the question in one clear sentence and used simple words. Removed technical jargon and kept the idea that waiting gives managers flexibility and value. Options are short, plausible, and preserve the original correct choice.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Real options embody managerial flexibility (deferring, expanding, abandoning, staging, contracting) in response to uncertainty, and this flexibility has measurable value.

Concept 2: Real options differ from financial options in market structure and uncertainty assessment: they are not traded securities, managers influence the option’s underlying value, and uncertainty is based on perception rather than volatility; options are created/discovered entrepreneurially.

Concept 3: The option premium concept in real options: the upfront non-recoverable expenditure that grants flexibility, linked to decisions about project size, timing, and operation, and operationalized through explicit assumptions and decision-support tools.

questions.question: Using $E = hf$, how does the light frequency $f$ decide if one photon can ionize an atom, and why is there a threshold? What happens to any extra energy when the photon is just above or well above that threshold?

options.A: Photon energy is $E = hf$. If $f$ is above the ionization threshold, one photon can eject the electron and any extra energy becomes the ejected electron's kinetic energy; below threshold it cannot ionize.
options.B: Ionization happens only when the photon energy exactly equals the ionization energy; any higher energy is wasted and cannot eject the electron.
options.C: Ionization depends on the field amplitude and on $f^2$ too; frequency alone does not determine whether ionization occurs.
options.D: An atom's ionization energy falls as frequency increases, so higher-frequency photons ionize more easily no matter their photon energy.

questions.simplification_notes: Split into two short sentences, used $E = hf$, removed advanced wording, kept the threshold and extra-energy ideas.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Photon energy depends on frequency/wavelength, and higher-energy photons can ionize atoms (ionizing vs nonionizing radiation).

Concept 2: Different bands of the electromagnetic spectrum are characterized by distinct production, interaction with matter, and applications, illustrating cause–effect relationships between frequency/wavelength and physical behavior.
Concept 3: Spectroscopy uses measurement of intensity as a function of frequency/wavelength to study how electromagnetic waves interact with matter.

questions.question: A small block of mass $m$ slides down a frictionless track from height $h_1$ to a lower height $h_2$ (where $h_2<h_1$). Why does its speed increase while total mechanical energy stays the same?

options.A: Gravity does positive work as the block falls, turning potential energy into kinetic so total stays constant.
options.B: Kinetic energy depends only on velocity and potential energy doesn't affect energy conservation.
options.C: An external agent injects energy during descent to increase the block's speed.
options.D: The gravitational field raises the block's rest energy as it loses height, increasing total energy.

questions.simplification_notes: Made sentences shorter and clearer for middle school. Converted variables to LaTeX ($m$, $h_1$, $h_2$). Kept core idea of energy conversion and conservation; options shortened but plausible.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Conservation of energy — energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed among forms (work, heat, light) within a closed system.

Concept 2: Relationship between kinetic and potential energy — total energy (often termed mechanical energy when considering these forms) is the sum of kinetic and potential energies, with transformations between them governing motion.

Concept 3: Variety and interconversion of energy forms — energy exists in multiple forms (chemical, radiant, internal, rest, etc.) and can be converted from one form to another, with real-world examples in biology and climate systems.

questions.question: Charged particles interact by exchanging virtual photons in QED. How does that give a relativistic coupling that becomes a Coulomb-like $1/r$ force at large distances, and what does a massless photon do to the force range?

options.A: The force results from instantaneous action at a distance. Massless photons imply immediate influence across frames.
options.B: The photon propagator in relativistic QFT gives a long-range $1/r$ potential for a massless mediator. If the mediator had mass, the potential would be Yukawa $e^{-mr}/r$, giving a finite range and preserving causality.
options.C: The force comes only from real photon emission and absorption events. Masslessness then somehow ensures energy conservation during those events.
options.D: Photons carry electric charge, so exchanging them changes particle charges. Masslessness supposedly prevents self-energy corrections.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened sentences and used simpler words. Kept core physics terms: photon propagator, $1/r$ Coulomb form, Yukawa $e^{-mr}/r$ for massive mediator, and relativistic causality.
concepts_tested: Photons as mediators of electromagnetic interactions in QED (exchange of photons in a relativistic quantum field framework)
QED as the quantum counterpart of classical electromagnetism and its reconciliation with special relativity (relationship between theories)

Perturbation theory in QED and the problem of divergences in higher orders, including the conceptual idea of renormalization (adjusting mass/charge to finite experimental values) and the theory’s predictive successes (e.g., anomalous magnetic moment, Lamb shift)

questions.question: How do cheaper filmmaking tools and outside investment affect who can make movies and the film industry's structure?

options.A: They raise entry barriers, keeping production in big studios with centralized money.
options.B: They lower entry barriers, cutting costs so new producers can join and diversify film production.
options.C: They create a single global distribution model and remove regional production hubs.
options.D: They remove creative control, causing films to look the same everywhere.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the question to one sentence, used simpler words, and kept the core idea that cheaper tech plus outside funding change who can produce films and the industry's shape.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Production costs and access to capital influence industry structure, driving consolidation around major studios while enabling independent production when barriers are lowered.
Concept 2: Technological democratization (affordable filmmaking equipment) and external investment act as mechanisms that broaden entry points for new producers and reshape the production ecosystem.

Concept 3: Global market geography (regional box-office leadership and production hubs) shapes where films are produced and how revenue flows, reflecting the interplay between markets and production centers.

questions.question: In a systems-thinking view, why can a policy that helps the economy in the short run lead to very different long-term results? What main mechanism causes this?

options.A: Because only official rules and laws matter; culture does not change.
options.B: Because any cultural changes are quickly erased by external shocks and leave no long-run effect.
options.C: Because the policy changes beliefs and habits, which feed back into behavior and create feedback loops and path-dependent outcomes.
options.D: Because long-run outcomes are fixed by initial resources and do not respond to policy or culture.

questions.simplification_notes: Made two short sentences, used simpler words, kept 'feedback loops' and 'path-dependent' idea, kept correct answer C.
concepts_tested: Culture influences economic outcomes through mechanisms such as social capital, social networks, social learning, and information cascades.
Decision making in cultural economics is path-dependent, guided by long-standing trajectories and regularities formed over time.
A systems-thinking framework treats culture and the economy as interdependent with feedback loops, incorporating value systems and cultural transmission (e.g., MEMEnomics).

questions.question: In the M-E-S framework, why do scientists cycle between the model (M), experiment (E), and system (S)?

options.A: Because experiments alone fully show how S works, so models are not needed.
options.B: Because testing and updating M with E data fixes wrong predictions, tunes parameters, and guides new experiments.
options.C: Because M, E, and S must stay separate and never influence each other.
options.D: Because computers can simulate S perfectly without experiments, making E and S checks pointless.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened sentences, simpler words, and kept the core idea: the iterative loop updates models with experimental data to improve understanding.
concepts_tested: The essence and methodology: computational science uses mathematical models and numerical algorithms implemented on computers to understand and solve complex problems, complementing theory and experimentation.
The M-E-S framework: a model (M) for a system (S) and an experiment (E) applied to answer questions about S, highlighting the conceptual relationships among data, models, and experiments.
Role of computation infrastructure: reliance on high-performance computing, including supercomputers and distributed platforms, to perform large-scale simulations and calculations.

questions.question: Why must a derivative's price equal the cost of a self-financing portfolio that exactly copies its payoff? How does this stop risk-free profit and relate to hedging risk?

options.A: Because the derivative's price equals its expected payoff under the real-world probability, regardless of hedging.
options.B: Because a price gap lets traders make risk-free profit. No-arbitrage forces the derivative price to equal the replication cost and hedging removes the risk.
options.C: Because the derivative depends only on the current underlying price, not future states or hedging.
options.D: Because no-arbitrage allows persistent price gaps, so hedge ratios must be constantly adjusted.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened to two clear sentences, used simpler words, removed advanced terms like 'risk-neutral measure', and kept the core idea that no-arbitrage forces equality with the replicating portfolio and links to hedging.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: The distinction and relationships between general equilibrium asset pricing (pricing via market clearing and macro variables) and rational/arbitrage-free pricing (unique risk prices per asset; low dimension).
Concept 2: The role of discounting expected cash flows at rates that reflect risk (undiversifiable risk) to determine present value.
Concept 3: The no-arbitrage principle in derivatives pricing and the linkage between derivative prices and the prices of underlying assets.

questions.question: How do universal design and accessibility differ in their goals? Why might universal design still leave some people needing assistive tech or extra changes?

options.A: Universal design guarantees one solution for everyone, so accessibility and assistive tech are not needed.
options.B: Universal design aims for one broad solution. Accessibility removes barriers and makes assistive tech work, so some people still need indirect access.
options.C: They are the same idea, so using one covers every user's needs.
options.D: Accessibility only means physical ramps, not digital tools, so universal design is enough for online use.

questions.simplification_notes: Split the original complex question into two short sentences. Used simpler words and kept the idea of universal design versus accessibility, including direct vs indirect access and the role of assistive technology.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Direct vs. indirect access and the role of assistive technology in achieving usable access.
Concept 2: The relationship between accessibility, usability, and universal design (different aims, shared goals).
Concept 3: Accessibility as a rights-based, policy-influenced practice with broader societal benefits (eliminating digital/knowledge divides).

questions.question: Why does testing a design many times with real users at different stages make design decisions better?

options.A: Because it guarantees the first requirements are perfect and never need change.
options.B: Because each test shows real user problems and priorities, letting designers fix them before costly development.
options.C: Because it hands all decisions to developers after the first idea, cutting out users.
options.D: Because one early test proves the design works for all users and situations.

questions.simplification_notes: Made the question and answers shorter and clearer for middle school. Kept the idea that repeated tests reveal real user problems and let designers improve before expensive work.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Iterative, stage-spanning usability testing driven by user requirements to influence design decisions.
Concept 2: Designing around users’ cognitive processes, mental models, tasks, and environmental context to create intuitive products.
Concept 3: Core design principles rooted in cognitive science (e.g., simplifying task structure, making things visible/feedback evident, and ensuring correct mappings between actions and results).

questions.question: How do rules and norms for property rights and enforcement lower the costs of buying and making things?

options.A: They give perfect information and remove all risk.
options.B: They reduce cheating and uncertainty, so people can trade and specialize with predictable incentives.
options.C: They replace private deals with government orders, causing more bureaucracy and delays.
options.D: They only affect social life, not buying or producing goods.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the question to one clear sentence. Reworded choices into simple phrases. Kept core idea that rules cut opportunism and uncertainty to enable trade and specialization.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Institutions as humanly devised constraints that shape social interaction and economic performance (e.g., how rules and norms alter costs of exchange and production).

Concept 2: The formal–informal spectrum of institutions and the role of enforcement in maintaining coordination and compliance (e.g., how varying levels of formality and third-party enforcement affect stability).

Concept 3: Mutually related rights and obligations and collective enforcement as the basis of social order (e.g., how shared expectations and enforcement mechanisms sustain institutional functioning).

questions.question: Why can a constitution allow the government to limit property rights for public needs?

options.A: Because property rights are absolute and can never be limited.
options.B: Because public interest can justify limits if the restriction follows law, due process, and fair compensation.
options.C: Because the law may cancel property rights to boost the economy, without following legal procedures.
options.D: Because property is only a government privilege that can be taken away at any time without due process.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened sentences and simpler words; kept the legal idea that property rights can be limited for public interest when done by law with due process and compensation; options made plausible and concise.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Property rights are not absolute; they may be restricted for public need or general interest under established laws.
Concept 2: Ambiguity in who counts as property owner (individuals vs corporations) and what counts as protected property (consumption vs production), influencing interpretation and scope.

Concept 3: Variation across international and regional instruments (UDHR, ICCPR/ICESCR, ECHR, ACHPR) in recognizing and delimiting the right to property, reflecting universal vs regional normative frameworks.

questions.question: Why do law and economics scholars use efficiency to judge legal rules and predict which rules will be made?

options.A: To try to raise total social welfare by lowering transaction costs and aligning incentives, while noting enforcement and information limits.
options.B: Because efficiency always ensures fair distribution of wealth, so efficient rules are always chosen.
options.C: Because lawmakers mainly follow public opinion, and efficiency is just a cover.
options.D: Because only rules that achieve perfect competition last, and efficiency guarantees that outcome.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened and simplified wording, removed technical jargon, kept the core idea that efficiency links to social welfare, transaction costs, incentives, and enforcement/information limits.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Economic efficiency as a criterion for evaluating legal rules and predicting which rules will be promulgated
Concept 2: Two main analytical branches—neoclassical analysis of law (positive and normative) and institutional analysis focusing on law and governance
Concept 3: Application of microeconomic theory to explain the effects of laws and to analyze the impact of legal rules on outcomes

questions.question: Why does collective intelligence often make better decisions than any single part alone? It combines data, machines, and people and uses feedback to make timely knowledge.

options.A: Because every part does the same job, so errors are removed by uniformity.
options.B: Because feedback turns raw data into context-rich, just-in-time knowledge, so the group adapts and uses each part's strengths.
options.C: Because it reduces human judgment and relies mostly on automatic aggregation.
options.D: Because it separates data processing from decision-making to stop wrong information from spreading.

questions.simplification_notes: Split the complex sentence into two short sentences, used simpler words (machines for software-hardware), and kept key idea about feedback and synergies.
concepts_tested: Emergence and synergistic interaction: collective intelligence arises from the combined synergies of data-information-knowledge, software-hardware, and individuals, with feedback leading to just-in-time knowledge and better decisions than any component alone.

Frameworks and mechanisms for analyzing/enhancing CI: functional elements (observation, prediction, creativity, judgement), learning loops, and organizational forms to diagnose and improve CI in cities, businesses, NGOs, or parliaments.

Distributed, networked cognition and mutual recognition: CI as a product of networked ICTs, social capital, collaboration (and sometimes competition), and the shift of knowledge/power toward the collective, including human–machine symbiosis.

questions.question: In a team with people from different jobs, leaders use clear rules like roles, communication protocols, and scheduled reflection. Why does mixing these rules with an equal, social leadership style help the team solve problems?

options.A: It centralizes authority to speed decisions and suppress conflict.
options.B: It forces rigid steps that lock the team into one solution early.
options.C: It lowers social friction and shares influence, so members share information, align ideas, and act together.
options.D: It ends the need for ongoing talk by writing everything down up front.

questions.simplification_notes: Rewrote as two short sentences, explained 'cross-functional' as 'team with people from different jobs', kept structured methods and social/egalitarian leadership, and made options concise and plausible.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Collaboration as a purposeful, strategic relationship among multiple actors aimed at a shared outcome (not just a loose association).
Concept 2: Structured collaboration methods and social/egalitarian leadership influence success by improving behavior, communication, and collaborative problem-solving.
Concept 3: Collaboration enables value creation and access to resources in competitive contexts; trade is a form of collaboration that leverages comparative advantages to benefit participants.

questions.question: Supply shifts right when production costs fall. If demand stays the same, why is the new equilibrium price lower and quantity higher?

options.A: Consumers value the good more, so demand increases and causes the change.
options.B: Producers can sell more at each price, so they lower the price to sell extra output.
options.C: At the original price there is a surplus (supply > demand), so price falls until $Q_d = Q_s$, raising quantity.
options.D: A government rule forces a lower price when supply rises, no matter the demand.

questions.simplification_notes: Split into two short sentences, removed technical jargon, kept key idea and used $Q_d = Q_s$ math notation.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Market equilibrium and market-clearing price/quantity (the intersection of supply and demand where Qd = Qs and price adjusts to clear the market).
Concept 2: The marginal-cost rule for supply under perfect competition (firms produce up to the point where price equals marginal cost).
Concept 3: How shifts in supply (e.g., due to changes in production costs) affect equilibrium price and quantity (left/right shifts and the resulting price/quantity changes).

questions.question: Why does higher demand for a skill raise its market wage, and what happens to how workers are allocated?

options.A: Wages act like prices; higher demand raises wages and draws more skilled workers until supply equals demand, making allocation efficient.
options.B: Wages come only from bargaining power, so higher demand goes to profits and wages and allocation stay the same.
options.C: Employers pay higher wages to a few preferred workers, cutting total jobs and misallocating labor.
options.D: Government price controls push wages up when demand rises, which can protect equity but distort signals.

questions.simplification_notes: Split the original two-part question into two short sentences, removed technical language, and kept the core idea that wages act as price signals and attract labor until supply meets demand.
concepts_tested: The wage-labour relationship as a two-sided exchange, where the worker sells labor power and the employer gains the work product (ownership/usage) in return.
Market-determined wages as a mechanism that links supply and demand in the labor market to compensation.

Variability in the wage-labour relationship through employment/civil status and remuneration forms (full-time/part-time, indentured/serf, cash/in-kind/piece rates) that modify the meaning and mechanics of the relationship.

questions.question: If wages rise, working becomes more rewarding but people can also afford more leisure. Which statement explains how hours worked actually change?

options.A: The substitution effect always wins, so higher wages always increase hours worked.
options.B: The income effect always wins, so higher wages always decrease hours worked.
options.C: Both effects can push in opposite directions; the net change depends on which effect is stronger for the person.
options.D: Wage changes have no effect on how many hours people work.

questions.simplification_notes: Made two short sentences; briefly explained substitution and income effects; kept technical terms simple and preserved the core idea that the net change depends on which effect dominates.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Rational labor supply decision — workers choose how much to work based on wage, leisure, and utility to maximize lifetime utility.
Concept 2: Derived labor demand and firm profit maximization — firms hire labor to maximize profits, with demand shaped by productivity and wages.
Concept 3: Human capital and schooling — investments in education increase productivity and influence earnings and labor-market outcomes.

questions.question: How do 'space of flows' and 'timeless time' change how work and social life are organized in a network society? 'Space of flows' means activities across distances without physical presence, and 'timeless time' means tasks can happen at different times.

options.A: Because digital networks let activities flow across distances without people being together, and time can be asynchronous.
options.B: Because physical closeness stays essential for coordination, and networks mainly replace people with automated systems.
options.C: Because economic activity becomes local and locked to fixed schedules again.
options.D: Because all flows happen instantly and are experienced the same in real time everywhere.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the question to one clear sentence and added a second sentence that defines 'space of flows' and 'timeless time'. Made all answer choices short and plausible.
concepts_tested: Space of Flows and Timeless Time as structural mechanisms reconfiguring social/economic activity (distance-less interaction and asynchronous timing).
Transition from industrial production to a knowledge-based economy driven by information flows and global networks.
Networked individualism and networks of networks shaping community, work, and organizational relations beyond physical proximity.

questions.question: How do friendships and healthy habits help people flourish instead of only giving short-term pleasure? "Flourish" means lasting well-being and a sense of meaning.

options.A: They only give short-term pleasure and do not support long-term growth.
options.B: They work together: social support lowers stress and helps coping. Shared activities build belonging and purpose, and exercise improves mood and thinking, creating lasting well-being.
options.C: They mainly make people follow social rules so they fit in and succeed.
options.D: They let people rely on others and avoid using self-control.

questions.simplification_notes: Made the question shorter and clearer. Defined "flourish." Broke complex ideas into short sentences and used simpler words while keeping the main mechanisms.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Hedonic vs. eudaimonic happiness and flourishing as the aim of well-being (why/how these are distinct and how they relate to positive psychology’s goals).
Concept 2: Strengths-based approach and transformative mechanisms (optimism, resilience, gratitude) as core drivers of well-being (how focusing on strengths leads to positive outcomes).

Concept 3: Relationships and lifestyle factors as mechanisms for well-being (role of social ties, community involvement, exercise, mindfulness/spiritual practices) and how they contribute to flourishing.

questions.question: What do logos, ethos, and pathos each do to persuade people? Why can a message that uses only logos (logic and facts) fail with different audiences?

options.A: Logos alone makes people believe by giving correct facts; ethos and pathos do not matter.
options.B: Logos gives evidence and reasons. Ethos builds trust and pathos moves emotions, so together they reach thinking, trust, and motivation.
options.C: Pathos alone drives persuasion; logos and ethos are mostly ceremonial and do not change beliefs.
options.D: Ethos makes the speaker seem authoritative, logos shows power, and pathos forces immediate obedience without evidence.

questions.simplification_notes: Split into two short sentences; explained logos in parentheses; kept simple words and short answer choices while preserving the triad and the limits of logos alone.
concepts_tested: The triad of persuasive appeals (logos, ethos, pathos) as a framework for influencing audiences and the mechanisms by which each appeal operates.
The five canons of rhetoric (invention, arrangement, style, memory, delivery) as sequential stages in crafting persuasive discourse and how each stage contributes to effectiveness.
The scope and adaptability of rhetoric across domains and cultures, highlighting its conceptual relation to logic, ethics, politics, and various fields of discourse.

questions.question: If a project's cash flows stay the same, why can using loans (debt) or selling shares (equity) still change how much value the project creates?

options.A: Because the financing choice changes the project's actual operating cash flows.
options.B: Because debt changes the risk and the rate used to value cash flows. Borrowing gives tax savings ('tax shield') but adds bankruptcy costs, so the project's net value can change.
options.C: Because debt always lowers the cost of capital and always raises the project's value, no matter the risk.
options.D: Because equity always removes all risk and therefore always raises the project's value.

questions.simplification_notes: Wording shortened to two clear sentences; technical terms 'tax shield' and 'bankruptcy costs' briefly explained; split complex idea into two short sentences for option B.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: The objective principle — corporate finance aims to maximize shareholder value, guiding all investment and financing decisions.

Concept 2: The capital allocation mechanism — capital budgeting (which value-adding projects to fund) and financing choices (debt vs. equity) determine how resources are allocated and how value is created.

Concept 3: The working capital mechanism and liquidity relationships — managing short-term assets and liabilities (cash, inventories, short-term borrowing) to sustain operations and influence overall firm value.

questions.question: How do diffraction experiments let scientists find atom positions and electron density in crystals? What main problem must be solved to get a unique electron-density map?

options.A: Because diffraction intensities give electron density directly, so no extra information is needed.
options.B: Diffraction measures amplitudes $|F(hkl)|$ but not the phases $\phi(hkl)$, so phasing methods are needed.
options.C: Because diffraction shows atom locations but not how electrons spread, you must assume electron shapes.
options.D: Electron density can be worked out from composition and symmetry alone, without diffraction data.

questions.simplification_notes: Split into two short sentences, used simpler words, kept key terms (amplitudes and phases) in LaTeX, and kept the original correct answer.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Diffraction data as the means to determine atomic positions and electron density.
Concept 2: The relationship between crystal structure/geometry and material properties across scales, including nonperiodic or quasicrystalline cases.
Concept 3: The historical/mechanistic linkage between new measurement techniques (X-rays, electron diffraction) and methodological advances (phasing algorithms) that enabled modern crystallography.

questions.question: Why does transfer happen more when a training task and a new situation share common elements?

options.A: Training builds one abstract skill that fits any task.
options.B: Because the tasks share cues, steps, and structures, so the same learned responses can be applied.
options.C: Because transfer requires repeating the exact same task word-for-word.
options.D: Because transfer does not depend on similarity and relies only on general intelligence.

questions.simplification_notes: Made the question shorter and used simpler words. Options kept the same ideas but phrased clearly for middle school students.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Identical elements theory — transfer depends on how similar the learning task and the transfer task are, i.e., the presence of common elements between contexts.

Concept 2: Promoting transfer through curriculum design — instructional strategies should include tasks that resemble real-world contexts or train in conditions similar to where performance will be required.

Concept 3: Transfer as a continuum integrated with learning — transfer is not always a separate, discrete event but is connected to learning itself, with debates about its boundaries and conceptualization.

questions.question: How do decomposers connect the physical breaking of dead plants and animals to the chemical release of minerals? Why is their work needed for decomposition to go through its normal stages?

options.A: They chemically convert organic matter straight into minerals, bypassing microbial steps.
options.B: They fragment and eat tissue, increasing surface area so microbes chemically break it down and release minerals.
options.C: They mainly act as scavengers that remove tissues, reducing nutrients and slowing decomposition.
options.D: They act only after chemical breakdown is finished, so they do not affect decay speed or stages.

questions.simplification_notes: Wording shortened and split into two short sentences; technical terms reduced; preserved idea that decomposers fragment matter to help microbes release minerals and drive decay stages.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Abiotic vs biotic decomposition as distinct mechanisms that convert organic matter into simpler substances.
Concept 2: Autolysis and putrefaction as the initiating biological processes in animal decomposition, and their chemical byproducts (e.g., cadaverine, putrescine) and odor.
Concept 3: The role of decomposers/detritivores (bacteria, fungi, insects, scavengers) in driving decomposition and linking it to the nutrient cycle and the staged progression of decay.

questions.question: Why does comparative advantage mean both countries can be better off with free trade?

options.A: Because each country shifts resources to the good it produces with the highest absolute productivity.
options.B: Because each country specializes in the good with the lower opportunity cost. Trade lets both consume more than they could produce alone.
options.C: Because removing trade barriers makes prices identical across countries, so production choices are not distorted.
options.D: Because free trade forces one country to become self-sufficient, eliminating the need to trade.

questions.simplification_notes: Simplified wording, shorter sentences, split complex idea into two short sentences for the correct option, kept focus on comparative advantage and gains from specialization and trade.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Comparative advantage as the mechanism by which free trade can raise economic welfare through efficient resource allocation.
Concept 2: Policy instruments and barriers (tariffs, quotas, subsidies, non-tariff barriers) shape trade flows and distort markets, distinguishing free trade from protectionism.

Concept 3: Long-run vs short-run welfare effects of liberalization (positive growth/stability in the long run, with potential short-run losses and dislocation for workers in import-competing sectors).

questions.question: How does the WTO's binding dispute settlement system help countries follow the rules and make trade more predictable?

options.A: It imposes automatic penalties in every case, guaranteeing compliance.
options.B: It issues time-bound, rule-based rulings and allows retaliation if rules are ignored. This creates credible enforcement and predictability.
options.C: All members must approve every ruling, which would slow decisions and raise uncertainty.
options.D: It only covers tariffs, so it has little effect on wider trade flows.

questions.simplification_notes: Simplified wording to one clear question. Options shortened to short sentences. Kept core idea that binding, time-bound rulings and possible retaliation improve enforcement and reduce uncertainty.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Non-discrimination principle (Most-Favoured-Nation and national treatment) and its exceptions; how these rules shape how countries treat each other in trade, and why exceptions exist.
Concept 2: Dispute settlement and negotiation mechanisms as the enforcement and rule-making backbone of the WTO; how these mechanisms influence compliance, dispute resolution, and trade flow.

Concept 3: WTO governance and decision-making structure (Ministerial Conference, General Council, Secretariat) and its impact on negotiation outcomes, legitimacy, and policy effectiveness (e.g., Doha Round dynamics, Appellate Body issues).

questions.question: Why does a two-dimensional shape with a line of symmetry always match its mirror image?

options.A: A: A reflection across the axis reverses left and right. It maps the shape onto itself, so the mirror matches.
options.B: B: The axis lets you rotate the shape to match its mirror image.
options.C: C: The axis means the mirror image cannot be put on top of the original.
options.D: D: You need two perpendicular symmetry axes in 2D for achirality.

questions.simplification_notes: Made the wording shorter and clearer for middle-school level. Kept the idea that a reflection across the symmetry axis maps the figure onto itself, causing achirality.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Chirality as non-superposability under rotations/translations leading to enantiomorphs; mirror images that cannot be aligned.

Concept 2: Relationship between symmetry and chirality: achirality occurs when the figure’s symmetry group includes orientation-reversing isometries; presence of an axis of symmetry in 2D implies achirality.
Concept 3: Real-world and mathematical manifestations of chirality (hands, helices, knots, tilings) as demonstrations of handedness and symmetry-based classification.

questions.question: Why does one enantiomer form more than the other in enantioselective synthesis?

options.A: Both pathways have the same activation energy; selectivity comes from steps after the enantiomers form.
options.B: Two competing transition states have different activation energies. The lower-energy state gives the major enantiomer and is stabilized by a chiral environment.
options.C: The catalyst forces one single product shape no matter which path the reaction takes.
options.D: Only the substrate's shape decides which enantiomer forms; the catalyst or environment has no effect.

questions.simplification_notes: Question shortened and wording simplified for middle school students; technical terms kept but explained in options. Removed equations and detailed thermodynamic wording while keeping the idea of different transition-state energies and chiral stabilization.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Asymmetric induction biases product formation by lowering the activation energy for one enantiomer’s pathway, making its formation faster than the other.

Concept 2: The enantiodifferentiating step, i.e., the step at which the reaction could yield two enantiomeric products, determines selectivity through different transition-state energies and hence different rate constants.
Concept 3: The source of chirality that enables enantioselectivity can be in the substrate, reagent, catalyst, or environment, all contributing to the chiral influence on the reaction outcome.

questions.question: Gettier cases show a justified true belief can be true by luck. What does this mean for knowledge?

options.A: Justification must be infallible to prevent luck.
options.B: Even with good justification, a true belief can be due to luck. Knowledge needs an extra anti-luck link between belief and truth.
options.C: Justification is irrelevant; knowledge is only whether the belief is true.
options.D: Knowledge is just justified true belief; Gettier cases show nothing about justification.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened sentences and simpler words; kept the main idea that Gettier cases show true justified beliefs can be lucky and that knowledge needs an extra anti-luck condition.
concepts_tested: The role and nature of justification in turning true belief into knowledge, and the debates about what counts as adequate justification
The main theories of justification—foundationalism, coherentism, and infinitism—and their implications for the structure of knowledge
The Gettier problem and its impact on defining knowledge beyond true belief with justification

questions.question: Why does the Moon make two tidal bulges in Earth's oceans?

options.A: The Moon pulls equally on all ocean water, so bulges come only from air pressure and Earth's rotation.
options.B: The Moon pulls strongest on the near side, making a bulge. The far side is pulled less than Earth's center, so it makes a second bulge.
options.C: The Sun's gravity is the main cause, so tides happen only from the Sun.
options.D: Because water is incompressible, gravity pulls it under the Moon and makes a single bulge directly beneath it.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the question, used simple words, split complex ideas into two short sentences, and removed technical terms like 'gradient'.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Tides arise from gravitational forces of the Moon and Sun, illustrating a cause–effect link between celestial dynamics and Earth’s oceanic response.
Concept 2: Measurements of gravity, magnetic, and electromagnetic fields reveal Earth's internal structure and dynamic processes, linking observable fields to underlying geology and tectonics.

Concept 3: Geophysics integrates multiple physical processes (Earth’s interior, hydrosphere, atmosphere, ionosphere, and space environment) using quantitative methods to study their interactions and collective behavior.

questions.question: Why does connecting a country's measurement system to international bodies like the BIPM and the SI make trade fair?

options.A: By forcing every country to use the same techniques and reach perfect accuracy.
options.B: By giving a shared reference system (the SI) and a traceability chain. This links national measurements to agreed international standards so they compare fairly.
options.C: By making one international laboratory do all calibrations and certify every measurement.
options.D: By letting countries ignore local rules and only use international standards to cut costs.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the question and used simpler words. Kept the core idea: shared SI reference, traceability links, and cross-country comparability for fair trade.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: The triadic metrology activities — defining units, realising those units in practice, and establishing traceability to reference standards — and their interdependence.
Concept 2: The metrology infrastructure and governance — national measurement systems connected to international bodies (BIPM, SI) and how this linkage enables consistent measurements and fair trade.

Concept 3: The three sub-fields of metrology — scientific/fundamental, applied/industrial, and legal metrology — and how they conceptually connect measurement science with regulation, practice, and policy.

questions.question: Why did the printing press let news be spread more widely and on a regular schedule than handwritten notices?

options.A: It let people print many identical copies cheaply, cutting cost per copy and enabling regular wide distribution.
options.B: It made production faster but did not change how news was shared.
options.C: It automatically made everyone literate and suddenly created mass demand for print.
options.D: It gave authorities centralized control so all printed news matched official views.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the question, used simple words, kept one clear sentence. Options were made brief and plausible while preserving the original answer.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Technology-driven expansion of news dissemination and public access (e.g., printing press enabling broader, regular distribution).
Concept 2: Evolution of news formats and the criteria that distinguish a "newspaper" (audience scope, regularity, accessibility) from earlier notices and pamphlets.
Concept 3: Interaction of literacy and oral culture in news transmission (low literacy leading to read-aloud dissemination and reliance on intermediaries).

questions.question: How can fixing climate change, reducing inequality, and improving government make a country safer? Why might these help more than relying only on military strength?

options.A: Because good governance and well-being help a nation resist and recover from non-military threats. Reducing climate risks and inequality builds unity and resilience beyond the battlefield.
options.B: Because a strong military deters outsiders, so internal reforms seem unnecessary.
options.C: Because this view says security is only economic growth, making defense irrelevant.
options.D: Because climate change and inequality do not affect security; only military force matters.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened sentences, used clear middle-school language, split the question into two simple sentences, and made each option brief but plausible. Kept the original core idea that governance and resilience reduce non-military security risks.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Multi-dimensional nature of national security (military and non-military threats such as terrorism, economy, energy, environment, cyber) and how these dimensions interact.

Concept 2: Mechanisms and instruments of security (political, economic, military power, diplomacy) and how they are used to safeguard a state, including regional/international cooperation and addressing root causes.
